image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of Matching Networks showing an embedding function f for the query, embedding function g with bidirectional LSTM for support set items, attention-weighted sum over support labels to produce a prediction

image_metadata.caption: Relation Network: learned relation module scores query-support embedding pairs.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after Matching Networks to illustrate the progression from fixed to learned distance functions, emphasizing the two-module design
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of Relation Network for a 5-way 1-shot problem showing an embedding module that encodes query and support images, concatenation of embedding pairs, and a relation module (CNN) that outputs a relation score

image_metadata.caption: Prototypical Networks: class prototypes as mean embeddings, few- and zero-shot.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining Prototypical Networks to illustrate how averaging embeddings creates class prototypes and how the same framework extends to zero-shot settings
image_metadata.description: Diagram of Prototypical Networks in both few-shot and zero-shot scenarios, showing how class prototype vectors are computed as mean embeddings of support examples, and how query points are classified by nearest prototype

image_metadata.caption: NTM architecture: controller with soft-attention read/write over external memory.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show before explaining MANN for meta-learning to ensure students understand the base NTM architecture and why external memory is advantageous over internal RNN memory
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a Neural Turing Machine (NTM) showing a controller network connected to an external memory matrix M_t of size N×M, with read and write heads using soft attention over memory rows

image_metadata.caption: MANN meta-learning: labels delayed by one step to force memory use.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how MANN is adapted for meta-learning, specifically to illustrate why the label-delay trick forces the network to use its external memory effectively
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the MANN meta-learning task setup showing how input x_t is presented at time t with the label y_{t-1} from the previous timestep, forcing the network to store inputs in memory and retrieve them when the delayed label arrives

image_metadata.caption: Slow + fast weights combined element-wise in a MLP for rapid adaptation.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing MetaNet's fast weight mechanism, before the full MetaNet architecture, to establish the conceptual building block of fast vs. slow weights
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a MLP combining slow weights (standard SGD-trained) and fast weights (generated by another network) via element-wise addition, showing how fast weights modulate the slow-weight network's activations

image_metadata.caption: MetaNet architecture: fast weights generated and injected for rapid task adaptation.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining fast weights and the individual MetaNet components, as a synthesis diagram to help students see how all parts connect in the full training loop
image_metadata.description: Full architecture diagram of MetaNet showing the base learner and meta-learner components, fast weight generation from loss gradients, task-level and example-level fast weight computation, and how they are injected back into the embedding and classification functions

image_metadata.caption: LSTM meta-learner updates learner parameters using gradient and loss signals.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining LSTM-based meta-learners to illustrate how the meta-learner receives gradient information and produces parameter updates, and how training mimics test-time conditions
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the interaction between the learner M_θ and the meta-learner R_Θ (LSTM-based optimizer), illustrating how the meta-learner receives gradients and loss from the learner and outputs updated parameters, across training episodes

image_metadata.caption: Meta-learner training: inner loop adapts, outer loop optimizes across tasks. (Finn et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the general meta-learning training paradigm before diving into specific algorithms like MAML; use to contrast inner-loop task adaptation with outer-loop meta-objective optimization.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the meta-learner training process: an outer loop samples tasks from meta-train distribution, an inner loop updates learner parameters over T steps on D_train, and the final parameters theta_T are evaluated on D_test to update the meta-learner.

image_metadata.caption: MAML finds an initialization enabling fast adaptation to any task. (Finn et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When first introducing MAML; show before the mathematical formulation to build geometric intuition about why a shared initialization is valuable across tasks.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of MAML showing a single initial parameter theta branching into multiple task-specific adapted parameters (theta_1*, theta_2*, theta_3*) after gradient steps, with the meta-update pulling theta toward a point that enables fast adaptation to all tasks.

image_metadata.caption: MAML pseudocode: inner gradient steps then second-order meta-update. (Finn et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the geometric intuition of MAML; use when students need to understand implementation details or when comparing to FOMAML to highlight where second derivatives appear.
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode of the general MAML algorithm showing: require p(tau), alpha, beta; sample tasks; for each task compute gradient and adapted parameters; update meta-parameters using test loss on adapted parameters with second-order gradients.

image_metadata.caption: Batched Reptile: sample tasks, run SGD k steps, update toward adapted weights. (Nichol et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing Reptile after MAML; highlight the algorithmic simplicity and ask students to identify what makes it different from plain SGD (the k>1 inner steps).
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode of the batched Reptile algorithm: initialize theta, repeatedly sample tasks, run k steps of SGD on each task to get W, then update theta toward W using step size epsilon.

image_metadata.caption: Reptile moves theta toward the intersection of task-optimal manifolds. (Nichol et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining the theoretical justification for Reptile; use to help students visualize why alternating task updates lead to a generalizable initialization rather than overfitting to any single task.
image_metadata.description: Geometric visualization showing two curved manifolds (W_tau1* and W_tau2*) representing optimal parameter regions for two tasks, with Reptile's iterative updates moving theta alternately closer to each manifold, converging toward their intersection.

image_metadata.caption: Reptile vs FOMAML: comparing meta-gradient directions in one update loop. (Lee, Yoonho)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When analyzing the theoretical connection between Reptile and FOMAML; use alongside the Taylor expansion derivation to help students see how the two algorithms relate geometrically and mathematically.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison diagram of Reptile vs FOMAML gradient updates in one meta-optimization loop, showing the direction and composition of gradient steps for each algorithm starting from the same initial theta.

image_metadata.caption: Randomized training images from Tobin et al. (2017) illustrating visual domain randomization.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing uniform domain randomization, especially when explaining why randomizing visual parameters helps policies generalize to unseen real-world appearances.
image_metadata.description: A grid of rendered images showing the same robotic manipulation scene with varied visual properties — different textures, lighting, colors, and backgrounds — demonstrating uniform domain randomization applied to training environments.

image_metadata.caption: Learning to Simulate: task feedback optimizes simulator parameters φ. (Ruiz, 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from uniform DR to optimization-based DR, to help students see how feedback from the target domain can replace hand-tuned randomization ranges.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart-style overview of the 'Learning to Simulate' framework, showing how a simulator parameterized by φ generates training data, a policy is trained on it, the policy is evaluated on a target task, and feedback is used to optimize the simulation parameters.

image_metadata.caption: SimOpt iteratively aligns simulator and real-world trajectory distributions. (Chebotar et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining methods that use real-world data to guide DR, contrasting it with purely simulation-based approaches like uniform DR or Learning to Simulate.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of the SimOpt framework showing an iterative loop: initial randomization distribution → policy training in sim → deployment on both sim and real robot → trajectory comparison → optimization of randomization distribution parameters.

image_metadata.caption: RCAN translates randomized or real images to canonical simulator views. (James et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing image-based domain adaptation methods, particularly to contrast the 'translate to canonical sim' strategy with the 'randomize sim to cover real' strategy of DR.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing RCAN's image-conditional GAN architecture: it takes a domain-randomized or real image as input and outputs a canonical non-randomized simulator image, which is then used for policy training.

image_metadata.caption: DeceptionNet uses adversarial deception modules to generate harder training images. (Zakharov et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when contrasting different objectives for guided DR — specifically to highlight the 'maximize difficulty' objective versus the 'maximize task performance' objective used in other methods.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of the DeceptionNet architecture showing deception modules (encoder-decoder networks) that transform input images with various randomizations, feeding into a downstream recognition/classification network whose loss signal trains the deception modules adversarially.

image_metadata.caption: ADR adaptively expands randomization bounds using trajectory similarity feedback. (Mehta et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining adaptive or curriculum-based domain randomization, to illustrate how the range of randomization parameters can be dynamically adjusted based on policy performance signals.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart of Active Domain Randomization (ADR) showing how a discriminator compares sim and real trajectories to measure environment similarity, which drives adaptive expansion or contraction of randomization parameter bounds during training.

lessons.title: Reinforcement Learning for Robotics
lessons.content:
# Reinforcement Learning for Robotics

Robots are unforgiving teachers. If your policy is a little wrong, the robot doesn’t “kind of” walk—it faceplants. Reinforcement learning (RL) is appealing here because it doesn’t require us to hand-code every reflex. Instead, we define a **reward** for “good behavior,” let the robot (usually in simulation) try things, and optimize the behavior that earns high long-term reward.

## The RL loop (robot edition)
At each time step, the robot observes a **state/observation** (joint angles, velocities, contacts, maybe camera features), chooses an **action** (torques or target positions), and receives a **reward**. Over a rollout (a **trajectory**), we sum discounted rewards into a **return**.

Two robotics-flavored details matter immediately:
- Actions are often **continuous** (torques), which pushes us toward policy gradient / actor-critic methods.
- Rewards are often **sparse** (“success = 1”), which is brutal for exploration—so we often use reward shaping.

## Policy gradients: learning by nudging probabilities
A **policy** \(\pi_\theta(a\mid s)\) is a neural net that outputs an action distribution. Policy gradient methods adjust \(\theta\) to make actions that led to high return more likely.

To reduce variance, we don’t just use “how good was this trajectory?” We use an **advantage**:
\[
A(s,a) = Q(s,a) - V(s)
\]
Intuition: “Was this action better than what I expected in this state?” That’s the learning signal that tends to work in high-dimensional control.

## PPO: stable on-policy learning for locomotion
**PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization)** is popular in robotics because it’s hard to break: it limits how much the policy can change per update using a clipped surrogate objective. That lets you do multiple epochs of minibatch updates on the same collected data without the policy drifting too far.

A practical workflow is: (1) collect rollouts from many parallel simulators, (2) compute advantages (often with GAE), (3) run several epochs of PPO updates.

![CartPole: CleanRL PPO training curve showing average episodic returns. (CleanRL)](/api/wiki-images/reinforcement-learning/images/docs-cleanrl-dev-rl-algorithms-ppo_049.png)

## SAC: off-policy learning with “curiosity” baked in
**SAC (Soft Actor-Critic)** optimizes a maximum-entropy objective: it wants high reward *and* high policy entropy. That entropy term encourages exploration and makes learning more robust.

Key practical difference vs PPO:
- PPO is typically **on-policy** (fresh data each update).
- SAC is **off-policy** (reuses past experience via a replay buffer), often improving sample efficiency—useful when simulation is expensive or when you’re mixing in real robot data.

![SAC algorithm with entropy regularization and fixed temperature α. (Lilian Weng, adapted)](/api/wiki-images/reinforcement-learning/images/lilianweng-posts-2018-04-08-policy-gradient_008.png)

## Reward shaping: the art of teaching without cheating
For robotics, the reward is your curriculum. Good shaping gives dense hints without changing the task’s intent.

Examples:
- **Locomotion**: forward velocity bonus, energy penalty, uprightness bonus, foot slip penalty.
- **Manipulation**: distance-to-grasp shaping, lift height bonus, orientation alignment, gentle contact penalties.

The danger: if you over-shape, the agent learns to “game” the reward (e.g., jittering to harvest velocity) instead of solving the real task.

## Minimal code: training a policy with SB3
```python
import gymnasium as gym
from stable_baselines3 import PPO

env = gym.make("CartPole-v1")
model = PPO("MlpPolicy", env, verbose=1)
model.learn(total_timesteps=50_000)

obs, _ = env.reset()
for _ in range(200):
    action, _ = model.predict(obs, deterministic=True)
    obs, reward, terminated, truncated, _ = env.step(action)
    if terminated or truncated:
        obs, _ = env.reset()
```

## Summary
Reinforcement learning for robotics is about optimizing a policy through trial and error, using value/advantage estimates to make learning signal usable in high-dimensional control. PPO is a go-to for stable on-policy training (great for locomotion in sim), while SAC often wins on sample efficiency via off-policy replay and entropy-driven exploration—both rely heavily on thoughtful reward shaping.

## Recommended Reading
- [Key Concepts in RL (Spinning Up)](https://spinningup.openai.com/en/latest/spinningup/rl_intro.html) — crisp definitions of states, policies, trajectories, and returns.
- [Policy Gradient Algorithms](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2018-04-08-policy-gradient/) — intuitive walkthrough of PPO/SAC-style actor-critic methods.
- [Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/1707.06347) — the original PPO paper, short and foundational.
- [Learning Dexterous In-Hand Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/1808.00177) — what it looks like when RL meets real dexterous hardware.
lessons.summary: Reinforcement learning trains robots by optimizing a policy from trial-and-error experience, using advantage-based policy gradients to make learning stable. PPO emphasizes safe, bounded policy updates, while SAC improves exploration and sample efficiency with entropy regularization and off-policy replay—both depend critically on reward shaping.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo.py vs. OpenAI Baselines PPO: average episodic returns over training steps.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching students how to validate a new RL implementation — use it to discuss the importance of reproducing baseline results before claiming novelty.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns over training steps for CleanRL's ppo.py compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO across multiple classic control or MuJoCo environments, plotted against environment steps.

image_metadata.caption: Ant locomotion: CleanRL PPO vs. rl_games PPO average episodic returns.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When contrasting PPO performance across environments of varying complexity, or when discussing continuous action space challenges.
image_metadata.description: Learning curve plot showing average episodic returns vs. training steps for the Ant locomotion environment in IsaacGym, comparing CleanRL PPO against rl_games PPO.

image_metadata.caption: Ant locomotion: CleanRL PPO vs. rl_games PPO performance over wall-clock time.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing practical deployment considerations and the cost of engineering optimizations in RL training pipelines.
image_metadata.description: Learning curve plot showing average episodic returns vs. wall-clock training time for the Ant locomotion environment in IsaacGym, comparing CleanRL PPO against rl_games PPO.

image_metadata.caption: CartPole: CleanRL PPO training curve showing average episodic returns.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing PPO benchmarks, starting with simple environments before progressing to complex ones, or when verifying implementation correctness.
image_metadata.description: Training curve showing average episodic returns vs. global steps for the CartPole environment, likely comparing CleanRL's PPO implementation against a baseline, used as a simpler reference benchmark alongside the IsaacGym tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Parallel ES-RL: workers share seeds to reconstruct noise cheaply. (Salimans et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Evolution Strategies as an alternative to MDP/TD-based RL, specifically when explaining how ES scales efficiently via parallelism and why shared random seeds reduce communication cost.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart showing a parallel Evolution Strategies RL algorithm where a master node distributes random seeds to multiple parallel workers, each worker evaluates a perturbed policy in the environment, and returns scalar rewards back to the master for parameter updates. Workers share random seeds to reconstruct Gaussian noise without transmitting full gradient vectors.

image_metadata.caption: PPG alternates policy and auxiliary phases to decouple training. (Cobbe et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Present after explaining the parameter-sharing trade-off in actor-critic methods; use the two-phase structure to show how PPG resolves the conflict between policy and value objectives
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for the PPG (Phasic Policy Gradient) algorithm showing two alternating training phases: a policy phase updating the policy network with PPO, and an auxiliary phase updating the value network with distillation loss to prevent policy drift

image_metadata.caption: PPG outperforms PPO in sample efficiency on Procgen. (Cobbe et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the PPG algorithm diagram to validate the claim that phase separation improves sample efficiency; prompt students to identify which environments benefit most
image_metadata.description: Bar chart or learning curve comparing mean normalized performance of PPG versus PPO across multiple Procgen benchmark environments, showing PPG achieving higher sample efficiency

image_metadata.caption: SAC algorithm with entropy regularization and fixed temperature α. (Haarnoja et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce after explaining the maximum entropy RL objective; walk through each update step to show how the entropy term modifies standard actor-critic updates
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm showing entropy-regularized policy optimization with soft Q-function updates, policy update via reparameterization, and fixed temperature parameter α balancing reward and entropy

image_metadata.caption: SAC with auto-tuned temperature α via dual gradient descent. (Haarnoja et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the fixed-temperature SAC algorithm; highlight the single added line updating α and explain why this resolves the brittleness of manual temperature selection across different tasks
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for SAC with automatically adjusted temperature, adding a gradient-based update rule for α that minimizes the difference between current entropy and a target entropy threshold, removing the need for manual temperature tuning

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo.py vs. OpenAI Baselines PPO: episodic returns vs. wall-clock time.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the distinction between sample efficiency and time efficiency, or when students ask why wall-clock time matters in addition to environment steps.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns for CleanRL's ppo.py plotted against wall-clock time (rather than environment steps), compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo_atari.py vs. OpenAI Baselines PPO on Atari: average episodic returns.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing PPO on image-based environments or when contrasting low-dimensional state spaces (classic control) with high-dimensional pixel inputs (Atari).
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns over training steps for CleanRL's ppo_atari.py compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO on Atari game environments.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo_atari.py vs. OpenAI Baselines on Atari: episodic returns vs. wall-clock time.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the computational overhead of convolutional networks in RL, or when comparing training time across different environment types.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns for CleanRL's ppo_atari.py plotted against wall-clock time, compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO on Atari environments.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo_continuous_action.py on dm_control: average episodic returns over training steps.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing PPO for continuous control tasks, or when contrasting discrete-action (Atari) and continuous-action (dm_control/MuJoCo) RL settings.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns over training steps for CleanRL's ppo_continuous_action.py across DeepMind Control Suite (dm_control) continuous-action environments.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo_continuous_action.py on dm_control: episodic returns vs. wall-clock time.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the steps-based chart (Image 5) to teach students how to interpret both sample efficiency and time efficiency for continuous control.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns for CleanRL's ppo_continuous_action.py plotted against wall-clock time across dm_control continuous-action environments.

image_metadata.caption: PPO on Gymnasium dm_control environments; note LQR tasks never terminate or truncate.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing environment wrappers, episode boundaries, or when students encounter environments that never terminate — use to highlight the importance of reading environment documentation.
image_metadata.description: Performance chart for ppo_continuous_action.py on Gymnasium-wrapped dm_control environments, including special note about lqr environments that are never terminated or truncated, comparing results across multiple tasks.

image_metadata.caption: MuJoCo v2 vs. v4 environments: ppo_continuous_action.py performance is roughly equivalent.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing reproducibility in RL or when students ask about differences between gym and gymnasium, or between MuJoCo v2 and v4 environments.
image_metadata.description: Comparison chart showing average episodic returns for ppo_continuous_action.py using MuJoCo v2 environments versus v4 environments in Gymnasium, demonstrating performance equivalence across versions.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo_atari_lstm.py vs. OpenAI Baselines PPO on Atari: episodic returns with LSTM.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing recurrent policies in RL, or when discussing how memory can help agents in partially observable or temporally dependent tasks.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns over training steps for CleanRL's ppo_atari_lstm.py (PPO with LSTM recurrent policy) compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO on Atari environments.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo_atari_lstm.py vs. OpenAI Baselines on Atari: episodic returns vs. wall-clock time.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside Image 9 to contrast sample efficiency with time efficiency for recurrent PPO, or when discussing the overhead of backpropagation through time (BPTT).
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns for CleanRL's ppo_atari_lstm.py plotted against wall-clock time, compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO on Atari environments.

image_metadata.caption: PPO with EnvPool: episodic return vs. environment steps across Atari games.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing PPO's empirical performance on Atari games and the impact of using EnvPool for faster environment simulation
image_metadata.description: Learning curves (episodic return vs. environment steps) for PPO with Atari EnvPool across multiple Atari games, showing performance benchmarks

image_metadata.caption: PPO with EnvPool: episodic return vs. wall-clock time across Atari games.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When contrasting sample efficiency vs. computational efficiency, or when explaining the practical speedup from vectorized environment libraries like EnvPool
image_metadata.description: Learning curves (episodic return vs. wall-clock time) for PPO with Atari EnvPool, showing training speed rather than sample efficiency

image_metadata.caption: PPO (JAX/XLA + EnvPool): episodic return vs. environment steps on Atari.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When comparing different PPO implementations or discussing the effect of JAX/XLA on learning performance (not just speed)
image_metadata.description: Learning curves (episodic return vs. environment steps) for PPO implemented with JAX/XLA and EnvPool across Atari games, benchmarked against baselines

image_metadata.caption: PPO JAX scan variant: learning curves showing hardware-dependent speed differences.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining JAX-specific optimizations like lax.scan and their trade-offs between compilation time and runtime performance
image_metadata.description: Learning curves (episodic return vs. environment steps) for PPO with JAX/XLA scan variant, comparing performance across contributors with different hardware

image_metadata.caption: PPO JAX scan variant: wall-clock learning curves with hardware variability noted.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the practical considerations of JAX compilation costs and how lax.scan reduces them in RL training loops
image_metadata.description: Learning curves (episodic return vs. wall-clock time) for PPO with JAX/XLA scan variant, highlighting hardware-dependent speed differences between contributors

image_metadata.caption: PPO on ProcGen environments: performance comparison across procedurally generated games.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing ProcGen as a generalization benchmark and comparing PPO's performance to baselines in non-Atari settings
image_metadata.description: Comparison plot of PPO performance on ProcGen environments, likely showing episodic return across multiple procedurally generated games

image_metadata.caption: ppo_atari_multigpu.py matches ppo_atari.py sample efficiency across Atari games. (CleanRL)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing multi-GPU RL training or discussing how to scale PPO without losing sample efficiency; useful for comparing single vs. multi-GPU setups.
image_metadata.description: A learning curve chart (episodic return vs. environment steps) comparing ppo_atari_multigpu.py against ppo_atari.py across Atari games, demonstrating equivalent sample efficiency between the single-GPU and multi-GPU implementations.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-GPU PPO achieves ~30% wall-time speedup over single-GPU on Atari. (CleanRL)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the computational benefits of multi-GPU RL training, or when students ask how much speedup can be expected from parallelizing PPO across GPUs.
image_metadata.description: A wall-clock time learning curve comparing ppo_atari_multigpu.py against ppo_atari.py, showing that the multi-GPU variant achieves approximately 30% faster training on the same hardware.

image_metadata.caption: CartPole: CleanRL PPO vs. rl_games PPO episodic returns over training steps.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the trade-off between code simplicity and performance in RL implementations, or when introducing PPO benchmarking methodology.
image_metadata.description: Learning curve plot showing average episodic returns vs. training steps for the CartPole environment, comparing CleanRL's ppo_continuous_action_isaacgym.py against Denys88/rl_games PPO baseline.

image_metadata.caption: CartPole: CleanRL PPO vs. rl_games PPO performance over wall-clock time.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining why training time is an important practical metric alongside sample efficiency, especially in GPU-accelerated simulation contexts.
image_metadata.description: Learning curve plot showing average episodic returns vs. wall-clock training time (in seconds/minutes) for the CartPole environment, comparing CleanRL PPO against rl_games PPO on an NVIDIA RTX A6000.

image_metadata.caption: Humanoid: CleanRL PPO vs. rl_games PPO average episodic returns over steps.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching about scaling RL to complex tasks or when discussing why optimized implementations outperform simpler ones on hard problems.
image_metadata.description: Learning curve plot showing average episodic returns vs. training steps for the Humanoid locomotion environment in IsaacGym, comparing CleanRL PPO against rl_games PPO.

image_metadata.caption: Humanoid: CleanRL PPO vs. rl_games PPO performance over wall-clock time.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing hardware requirements and time costs for training on complex robotics tasks, or when comparing implementation efficiency.
image_metadata.description: Learning curve plot showing average episodic returns vs. wall-clock training time for the Humanoid environment in IsaacGym, comparing CleanRL PPO against rl_games PPO on an NVIDIA RTX A6000.

image_metadata.caption: BallBalance: CleanRL PPO vs. rl_games PPO average episodic returns.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When illustrating PPO performance across diverse task categories, or when discussing how task structure affects the simplicity-performance trade-off.
image_metadata.description: Learning curve plot showing average episodic returns vs. training steps for the BallBalance environment in IsaacGym, comparing CleanRL PPO against rl_games PPO.

image_metadata.caption: BallBalance: CleanRL PPO vs. rl_games PPO performance over wall-clock time.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When comparing time-based performance metrics across multiple environments to build a comprehensive picture of implementation efficiency.
image_metadata.description: Learning curve plot showing average episodic returns vs. wall-clock training time for the BallBalance environment in IsaacGym, comparing CleanRL PPO against rl_games PPO.

image_metadata.caption: Anymal: CleanRL PPO vs. rl_games PPO average episodic returns over steps.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing RL applications in robotics or when showing how PPO performs on tasks with physical contact dynamics.
image_metadata.description: Learning curve plot showing average episodic returns vs. training steps for the Anymal quadruped locomotion environment in IsaacGym, comparing CleanRL PPO against rl_games PPO.

image_metadata.caption: Anymal: CleanRL PPO vs. rl_games PPO performance over wall-clock time.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When summarizing the overall time-efficiency comparison across all IsaacGym environments, or when concluding a discussion on implementation trade-offs.
image_metadata.description: Learning curve plot showing average episodic returns vs. wall-clock training time for the Anymal quadruped environment in IsaacGym, comparing CleanRL PPO against rl_games PPO on an NVIDIA RTX A6000.

image_metadata.caption: AllegroHand: CleanRL PPO vs. rl_games PPO episodic returns over training steps.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the performance gap between research-grade and production-optimized PPO implementations, or when introducing the AllegroHand benchmark task.
image_metadata.description: Training curve showing average episodic returns vs. global steps for the AllegroHand dexterous manipulation task, comparing CleanRL's ppo_continuous_action_isaacgym.py against rl_games PPO baseline.

image_metadata.caption: AllegroHand: CleanRL PPO vs. rl_games PPO episodic returns over wall-clock time.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching students to evaluate RL algorithms by both sample efficiency and real-world training time, especially in GPU-accelerated simulation contexts.
image_metadata.description: Training curve showing average episodic returns vs. wall-clock time (in seconds/minutes) for the AllegroHand task, comparing CleanRL PPO and rl_games PPO on an NVIDIA RTX A6000.

image_metadata.caption: AllegroHand (clipped config): CleanRL PPO vs. rl_games PPO over training steps.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the effect of reward normalization or clipping on PPO performance in high-dimensional manipulation tasks.

image_metadata.description: Training curve showing average episodic returns vs. wall-clock time for the ShadowHand task with a clipped/alternative configuration, comparing CleanRL PPO and rl_games PPO.
