image_metadata.caption: LSTM meta-learner updates learner parameters using gradient and loss signals.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining LSTM-based meta-learners to illustrate how the meta-learner receives gradient information and produces parameter updates, and how training mimics test-time conditions
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the interaction between the learner M_θ and the meta-learner R_Θ (LSTM-based optimizer), illustrating how the meta-learner receives gradients and loss from the learner and outputs updated parameters, across training episodes

image_metadata.caption: Meta-learner training: inner loop adapts, outer loop optimizes across tasks. (Finn et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the general meta-learning training paradigm before diving into specific algorithms like MAML; use to contrast inner-loop task adaptation with outer-loop meta-objective optimization.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the meta-learner training process: an outer loop samples tasks from meta-train distribution, an inner loop updates learner parameters over T steps on D_train, and the final parameters theta_T are evaluated on D_test to update the meta-learner.

image_metadata.caption: MAML finds an initialization enabling fast adaptation to any task. (Finn et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When first introducing MAML; show before the mathematical formulation to build geometric intuition about why a shared initialization is valuable across tasks.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of MAML showing a single initial parameter theta branching into multiple task-specific adapted parameters (theta_1*, theta_2*, theta_3*) after gradient steps, with the meta-update pulling theta toward a point that enables fast adaptation to all tasks.

image_metadata.caption: MAML pseudocode: inner gradient steps then second-order meta-update. (Finn et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the geometric intuition of MAML; use when students need to understand implementation details or when comparing to FOMAML to highlight where second derivatives appear.
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode of the general MAML algorithm showing: require p(tau), alpha, beta; sample tasks; for each task compute gradient and adapted parameters; update meta-parameters using test loss on adapted parameters with second-order gradients.

image_metadata.caption: Batched Reptile: sample tasks, run SGD k steps, update toward adapted weights. (Nichol et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing Reptile after MAML; highlight the algorithmic simplicity and ask students to identify what makes it different from plain SGD (the k>1 inner steps).
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode of the batched Reptile algorithm: initialize theta, repeatedly sample tasks, run k steps of SGD on each task to get W, then update theta toward W using step size epsilon.

image_metadata.caption: Reptile moves theta toward the intersection of task-optimal manifolds. (Nichol et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining the theoretical justification for Reptile; use to help students visualize why alternating task updates lead to a generalizable initialization rather than overfitting to any single task.
image_metadata.description: Geometric visualization showing two curved manifolds (W_tau1* and W_tau2*) representing optimal parameter regions for two tasks, with Reptile's iterative updates moving theta alternately closer to each manifold, converging toward their intersection.

image_metadata.caption: Reptile vs FOMAML: comparing meta-gradient directions in one update loop. (Lee, Yoonho)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When analyzing the theoretical connection between Reptile and FOMAML; use alongside the Taylor expansion derivation to help students see how the two algorithms relate geometrically and mathematically.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison diagram of Reptile vs FOMAML gradient updates in one meta-optimization loop, showing the direction and composition of gradient steps for each algorithm starting from the same initial theta.

image_metadata.caption: Three approaches to sim2real transfer: system ID, domain randomization, and domain adaptation.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on sim2real transfer to orient students to the landscape of approaches before explaining any single method in depth.
image_metadata.description: A conceptual diagram showing three parallel approaches to sim2real transfer: system identification, domain randomization, and domain adaptation, each illustrated as a pathway from simulator to real world.

image_metadata.caption: Randomized training images from Tobin et al. (2017) illustrating visual domain randomization.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing uniform domain randomization, especially when explaining why randomizing visual parameters helps policies generalize to unseen real-world appearances.
image_metadata.description: A grid of rendered images showing the same robotic manipulation scene with varied visual properties — different textures, lighting, colors, and backgrounds — demonstrating uniform domain randomization applied to training environments.

image_metadata.caption: Learning to Simulate: task feedback optimizes simulator parameters φ. (Ruiz, 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from uniform DR to optimization-based DR, to help students see how feedback from the target domain can replace hand-tuned randomization ranges.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart-style overview of the 'Learning to Simulate' framework, showing how a simulator parameterized by φ generates training data, a policy is trained on it, the policy is evaluated on a target task, and feedback is used to optimize the simulation parameters.

image_metadata.caption: SimOpt iteratively aligns simulator and real-world trajectory distributions. (Chebotar et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining methods that use real-world data to guide DR, contrasting it with purely simulation-based approaches like uniform DR or Learning to Simulate.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of the SimOpt framework showing an iterative loop: initial randomization distribution → policy training in sim → deployment on both sim and real robot → trajectory comparison → optimization of randomization distribution parameters.

image_metadata.caption: RCAN translates randomized or real images to canonical simulator views. (James et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing image-based domain adaptation methods, particularly to contrast the 'translate to canonical sim' strategy with the 'randomize sim to cover real' strategy of DR.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing RCAN's image-conditional GAN architecture: it takes a domain-randomized or real image as input and outputs a canonical non-randomized simulator image, which is then used for policy training.

image_metadata.caption: DeceptionNet uses adversarial deception modules to generate harder training images. (Zakharov et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when contrasting different objectives for guided DR — specifically to highlight the 'maximize difficulty' objective versus the 'maximize task performance' objective used in other methods.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of the DeceptionNet architecture showing deception modules (encoder-decoder networks) that transform input images with various randomizations, feeding into a downstream recognition/classification network whose loss signal trains the deception modules adversarially.

image_metadata.caption: ADR adaptively expands randomization bounds using trajectory similarity feedback. (Mehta et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining adaptive or curriculum-based domain randomization, to illustrate how the range of randomization parameters can be dynamically adjusted based on policy performance signals.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart of Active Domain Randomization (ADR) showing how a discriminator compares sim and real trajectories to measure environment similarity, which drives adaptive expansion or contraction of randomization parameter bounds during training.

image_metadata.caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: pretraining → SFT → RLHF. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an orientation map; return to it as a 'you are here' anchor when transitioning between phases
image_metadata.description: A three-phase diagram showing ChatGPT's development pipeline: pretraining on large data, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and RLHF with a reward model and PPO optimization

image_metadata.caption: Language models generate text token-by-token via next-token prediction. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing language models to students unfamiliar with how LLMs generate text; use before explaining why completion alone is insufficient and SFT is needed
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF demonstrating autoregressive text completion by a language model, showing how a model predicts and appends the next token step-by-step given a prompt context

image_metadata.caption: RedPajama vs. LLaMA: ~1 trillion tokens across diverse data sources. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the pretraining data bottleneck or when explaining why 'garbage in, garbage out' applies to LLMs; useful for grounding abstract scale claims
image_metadata.description: A bar chart comparing dataset compositions of RedPajama and LLaMA training data, showing token counts across sources like Common Crawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, and StackExchange, with totals around 1 trillion tokens

image_metadata.caption: Training data demand is outpacing new Internet data generation. (Villalobos et al., 2022 via Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing limitations of scaling pretraining data, or to motivate why RLHF and human feedback become critical when raw data supply is constrained
image_metadata.description: A line graph showing the projected growth of training dataset size versus the growth rate of new Internet data generation over time, illustrating that dataset demand will outpace supply within a few years

image_metadata.caption: Pretrained models may complete prompts unhelpfully; SFT steers toward preferred responses. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the SFT section to motivate why fine-tuning is needed; effective as a discussion prompt asking students which completion they would prefer and why
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing multiple possible completions for the prompt 'How to make pizza', contrasting undesirable completions (e.g., more questions, unrelated text) with the preferred instructional response, illustrating the motivation for SFT

image_metadata.caption: OpenAI's 40 InstructGPT labelers: educational backgrounds vary widely. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the human element of RLHF data collection; use to spark discussion about bias, representation, and whose preferences are being optimized
image_metadata.description: A chart or infographic showing the educational background distribution of OpenAI's 40 human labelers used to create InstructGPT demonstration data, likely showing degrees in various fields

image_metadata.caption: SFT uses demonstration labels; RLHF uses a reward model to score and optimize responses. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the transition from Phase 2 (SFT) to Phase 3 (RLHF) to highlight what changes structurally; useful for students who conflate fine-tuning with reinforcement learning
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison diagram contrasting the SFT training setup (prompt → demonstration response) with the RLHF training setup (prompt → model response → reward model score → policy update), highlighting the structural difference between the two phases

image_metadata.caption: Reward model loss decreases as the score gap between preferred and rejected responses grows. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the RM loss equation to provide geometric intuition; particularly useful for students with some math background who benefit from seeing loss landscape behavior
image_metadata.description: A graph visualizing the reward model loss function, showing how the loss behaves as the score difference between a preferred and a non-preferred response varies, illustrating the pairwise ranking objective

image_metadata.caption: OpenAI's InstructGPT labeling UI: labelers rank responses to train the reward model. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how comparison data is collected for the reward model; use to discuss the practical challenges of scaling human feedback and the 73% inter-labeler agreement statistic
image_metadata.description: A screenshot of OpenAI's internal labeling UI used to collect InstructGPT reward model training data, showing a prompt with multiple model responses that labelers score on a 1–7 scale and rank by preference

image_metadata.caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: SFT → Reward Model → RL fine-tuning. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an overview, and revisit when explaining the RL fine-tuning phase to anchor the KL divergence constraint in the broader pipeline.
image_metadata.description: A three-phase diagram showing the development pipeline of ChatGPT/InstructGPT: (1) Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), (2) Reward Model training, and (3) RL fine-tuning with the reward model, illustrating how each phase builds on the previous one.

image_metadata.caption: RLHF objective: maximize reward while penalizing KL divergence from SFT. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce after students understand the three-phase pipeline; use when explaining why the RL fine-tuning phase needs a constraint and how that constraint is mathematically encoded.
image_metadata.description: A mathematical equation displaying the RLHF objective function, showing the combined optimization target as a sum of the expected reward from the reward model minus a KL divergence penalty term that keeps the RL policy close to the SFT model.

image_metadata.caption: Schulman's proposal: use RL to force LLMs to admit uncertainty. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the limitations and unintended consequences of RLHF, specifically to set up the tension between Schulman's theoretical proposal and the InstructGPT empirical results.
image_metadata.description: A screenshot from John Schulman's talk proposing solutions to fix hallucination using RL, outlining his argument that LLMs know what they know and that RLHF could in principle be used to force models to only output information they are confident about.

image_metadata.caption: InstructGPT data: RLHF improves preference but worsens hallucination. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the Schulman hallucination-fix slide to highlight the gap between theory and practice, and when discussing evaluation metrics and the limitations of human feedback as a training signal.
image_metadata.description: A chart or figure from the InstructGPT paper showing that RLHF-trained models exhibit higher hallucination rates compared to SFT-only models, despite being preferred overall by human labelers across other quality dimensions.

image_metadata.caption: Multiple context types needed for a single customer support query. (Huyenchip, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce this when teaching why vanilla LLMs fail in production settings and before explaining RAG or prompt engineering strategies
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating the multiple types of context required to answer a customer support query, showing how user history, product information, and situational factors feed into an LLM response

image_metadata.caption: RAG Phase 1: chunking and indexing documents into a vector store. (Liu/LlamaIndex, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing RAG architecture, specifically when explaining the offline indexing phase before discussing retrieval and generation phases
image_metadata.description: A screenshot from Jerry Liu's LlamaIndex talk showing Phase 1 of RAG: the chunking/indexing pipeline, illustrating how documents are split into chunks and stored in a vector index for later retrieval

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo's architecture fuses visual and language features via cross-attention. (DeepMind)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when teaching multimodal model design, especially to contrast with text-only transformer architectures and explain how non-text modalities are integrated
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of DeepMind's Flamingo multimodal model, showing how visual features from a vision encoder are interleaved with text tokens and fed into a large language model via cross-attention layers

image_metadata.caption: Guanaco 7B approaches GPT-4 performance via QLoRA compression. (Dettmers et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the cost-performance tradeoff in LLM deployment and when introducing model optimization techniques like quantization and LoRA
image_metadata.description: A bar chart comparing Guanaco 7B's performance against ChatGPT (GPT-3.5) and GPT-4 on benchmark tasks, demonstrating that a small, optimized open-source model can approach proprietary model performance

image_metadata.caption: Monarch Mixer replaces attention with structured Monarch matrix operations. (Fu et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when discussing limitations of the Transformer architecture (quadratic attention complexity) and when surveying next-generation model architectures
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of Monarch Mixer, a non-Transformer model architecture using structured Monarch matrices for both sequence mixing and feature mixing, proposed as a hardware-efficient alternative to attention

image_metadata.caption: Timeline of photonic matrix multiplication advances across three key methods. (Huyenchip, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when teaching AI infrastructure and hardware constraints, particularly when discussing why new chip architectures are needed to scale LLMs cost-effectively
image_metadata.description: A timeline chart showing advances across three major methods in photonic matrix multiplication over time, illustrating the rapid progress in photonic computing as a potential GPU alternative for AI workloads

image_metadata.caption: InstructGPT labelers: no one over 65, predominantly Filipino and Bangladeshi. (Ouyang et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing RLHF and alignment, specifically to challenge the assumption that human feedback is neutral or representative of all users
image_metadata.description: A demographic breakdown chart of labelers used for InstructGPT's RLHF training data, showing age distribution, geographic origin (predominantly Filipino and Bangladeshi), and other demographic attributes

image_metadata.caption: OpenAssistant contributors: 90.5% male among 222 respondents. (OpenAssistant, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Pair with the InstructGPT demographics image when teaching data collection challenges in alignment; use to spark discussion on how to design more representative feedback collection
image_metadata.description: A self-reported demographics chart for contributors to the OpenAssistant dataset, showing that approximately 90.5% of the 222 respondents identify as male, with breakdowns of other demographic variables

image_metadata.caption: Chat as universal super-app interface in China — a decade of precedent. (Dan Grover, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when discussing LLM interface design and the question of whether chat is the right modality, to ground the discussion in real-world precedent from Asian markets
image_metadata.description: A visual showing chat as the universal interface for Chinese super-apps (e.g., WeChat), demonstrating that conversational UI for complex multi-domain tasks has been deployed at scale in Asia for over a decade

image_metadata.caption: NVIDIA NeVA: powerful multimodal model, but generic chat interface. (NVIDIA, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when discussing the gap between model capability and interface design in multimodal AI, prompting students to think about what a purpose-built multimodal UI should look like
image_metadata.description: A screenshot of NVIDIA's NeVA multimodal chatbot interface, showing a standard text-chat UI used to interact with a vision-language model, with an image input and text response displayed

image_metadata.caption: Non-English tokenization is less efficient, inflating token counts. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Surface this when teaching why building LLMs for non-English languages is a distinct engineering challenge, or when discussing tokenizer design and its downstream effects on model performance and cost.
image_metadata.description: A comparison of tokenization across different languages, likely showing how the same concept requires more tokens in non-English languages (e.g., Thai, Chinese, Arabic) compared to English, illustrating token inefficiency for non-Latin scripts.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention splits inputs across parallel heads. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the multi-head attention mechanism, before or alongside the mathematical formulation, to ground the equations in a concrete computational flow.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing multi-head scaled dot-product attention: input splits into h heads, each computing scaled dot-product attention in parallel, outputs concatenated and linearly projected.

image_metadata.caption: Vanilla Transformer encoder-decoder architecture with attention and feed-forward layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of any Transformer overview lesson as a reference map, and revisit when discussing individual components to show where they fit in the full architecture.
image_metadata.description: Full architecture diagram of the vanilla Transformer model showing the encoder stack (left) and decoder stack (right), including multi-head attention, feed-forward layers, add & norm, positional encoding, and cross-attention.

image_metadata.caption: Sinusoidal positional encoding heatmap for L=32, d=128; values range −1 to 1.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the self-attention permutation-invariance problem and before or alongside the sinusoidal formula, to help students visualize what the encoding actually looks like.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap visualization of sinusoidal positional encoding for sequence length L=32 and embedding dimension d=128, showing alternating sine/cosine patterns across positions and dimensions.

image_metadata.caption: RoPE encodes position by rotating token embeddings in 2D subspaces. (Su et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching advanced positional encoding methods, after students understand sinusoidal encoding, to contrast additive vs. rotational approaches.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE): shows how token embeddings at position i are rotated in 2D subspaces using a rotation matrix, with the rotation angle proportional to position.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer-XL extends context by caching previous segment hidden states. (Dai et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the context segmentation problem in vanilla Transformers and introducing Transformer-XL as a solution, to make the architectural difference immediately visible.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of training in vanilla Transformer vs. Transformer-XL with segment length 4, showing how Transformer-XL caches previous segment hidden states to extend the attention span across segments.

image_metadata.caption: Compressive Transformer uses regular and compressed memory for long context. (Rae et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining Transformer-XL, when introducing the Compressive Transformer as a further extension, to highlight the added compressed memory tier.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Compressive Transformer showing two memory tiers: recent past stored as regular memory and older past compressed into compressed memory, with the model attending to both alongside current input.

image_metadata.caption: Attention weights across memory positions show compressed memory is actively used. (Rae et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the Compressive Transformer architecture, as empirical evidence that compressed memory is actually used by the model and not ignored.
image_metadata.description: Line plot showing attention weights (with one standard deviation error bars) versus memory positions from oldest (left) to newest (right), comparing attention to regular memory vs. compressed memory in the Compressive Transformer.

image_metadata.caption: SPALM gates short-term hidden states with long-term k-NN memory. (Yogatama et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching non-differentiable external memory augmentation, after k-NN LM, to illustrate how retrieval-augmented memory integrates with recurrent hidden states.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of SPALM combining short-term memory (past hidden states from Transformer-XL style cache) with long-term memory (external k-NN key-value datastore), using a gating mechanism to merge both memory sources.

image_metadata.caption: Fine-tuning with k-NN memory matches training Memorizing Transformer from scratch. (Wu et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the Memorizing Transformer to emphasize its practical advantage: existing pretrained models can be augmented with memory without full retraining.
image_metadata.description: Bar chart comparing perplexity of vanilla Transformer, Memorizing Transformer trained from scratch, and a vanilla Transformer fine-tuned with k-NN memory, showing fine-tuning achieves comparable performance to training from scratch.

image_metadata.caption: ALiBi adds a per-head linear distance penalty to attention scores. (Press et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching distance-enhanced attention methods, after introducing the motivation for relative positional encoding, to contrast ALiBi's simple linear bias with more complex approaches like RoPE.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases): shows how a negative linear bias proportional to the distance between query and key positions is added to attention scores before softmax, with different slopes per head.

image_metadata.caption: ALiBi extrapolates to longer contexts better than sinusoidal, rotary, or T5 encodings. (Press et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing ALiBi and before or during comparison with other positional encoding schemes, especially when students ask why ALiBi was proposed or what problem it solves.
image_metadata.description: Line graphs comparing extrapolation performance of four positional encoding schemes (sinusoidal, rotary, T5 relative, ALiBi) when models trained on short context lengths are tested on longer sequences. ALiBi shows the flattest perplexity curve, indicating best extrapolation.

image_metadata.caption: Universal Transformer refines all positions in parallel across recurrent steps. (Dehghani et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Universal Transformer, particularly to contrast with standard multi-layer Transformers and to illustrate how recurrence operates over depth rather than sequence position.
image_metadata.description: Animated-style diagram showing how Universal Transformer repeatedly applies the same transformation to all positions in parallel across multiple recurrent steps, with arrows indicating iterative refinement of hidden states h^1 through h^T.

image_metadata.caption: Universal Transformer encoder-decoder with shared recurrent structure and cross-attention. (Dehghani et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the recurrence loop diagram (Image 2) to give students the full encoder-decoder picture, especially when discussing sequence-to-sequence tasks or comparing to standard Transformer architecture.
image_metadata.description: Simplified encoder-decoder diagram of Universal Transformer showing the shared recurrent structure in both encoder and decoder, with the decoder additionally attending to the final encoder representation h^T via cross-attention.

image_metadata.caption: Soft mask function smoothly gates attention by distance, enabling learnable span z. (Sukhbaatar et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the m_z formula to help students visualize how the piecewise linear function controls the effective attention span and why it is differentiable.
image_metadata.description: Plot of the soft masking function m_z(x) used in adaptive attention span, showing a smooth sigmoid-like curve that transitions from 1 to 0 around the learned parameter z, with the x-axis representing distance and y-axis the mask value in [0,1].

image_metadata.caption: Three adaptive depth classifier types: sequence-, token-, and token-label-specific exits. (Elabyad et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Depth-Adaptive Transformer and comparing it to CALM, or when discussing the design space of early-exit strategies in efficient inference.
image_metadata.description: Three-panel illustration comparing sequence-specific, token-specific, and token-and-label-specific depth classifiers in Depth-Adaptive Transformer, showing how exit decisions are made at different granularities across layers.

image_metadata.caption: 1D and 2D local attention spans for image pixels in Image Transformer. (Parmar et al. 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing fixed local context attention, especially to contrast 1D sequence-based and 2D spatial attention patterns, or when discussing how Transformers are adapted for image generation.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side diagrams of 1D and 2D local attention patterns for image pixels in Image Transformer. Black outlines mark query blocks; cyan outlines show the actual attention span for a query pixel q, illustrating how context is restricted locally.

image_metadata.caption: Full, strided, and fixed sparse attention patterns and their connectivity matrices. (Child et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Sparse Transformer to contrast with full self-attention, and to help students understand the structural difference between strided and fixed sparse patterns.
image_metadata.description: Six-panel figure with top row showing attention connectivity graphs for (a) full Transformer, (b) Sparse Transformer with strided attention, and (c) Sparse Transformer with fixed attention, and bottom row showing corresponding sparse attention matrices.

image_metadata.caption: Attention patterns of ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird: global tokens plus local windows.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird to help students identify commonalities and differences, or as a summary visual after covering all three models.
image_metadata.description: Three-panel diagram showing attention patterns for ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird models, each illustrating how global tokens interact with all positions while local tokens use windowed attention, with Big Bird also including random attention.

image_metadata.caption: LSH attention matrix: queries attend only within the same hash bucket. (Kitaev et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the LSH process diagram (Image 10) to connect the algorithmic steps to the resulting attention matrix structure, especially when students struggle to see why LSH produces valid approximate attention.
image_metadata.description: Matrix visualization of LSH attention showing how queries and keys are grouped into hash buckets, sorted, and chunked, with the resulting sparse attention matrix highlighting which positions attend to each other within the same bucket.

image_metadata.caption: LSH attention: four steps — bucketing, sorting, chunking, attention computation. (Kitaev et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first introducing LSH attention in Reformer, walking through each step sequentially before showing the resulting attention matrix (Image 9).
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step process diagram of LSH attention showing four stages: (1) hashing queries and keys into buckets, (2) sorting by bucket, (3) chunking into fixed-size segments, and (4) computing attention within chunks.

image_metadata.caption: Linformer projects keys/values to rank-k, achieving linear inference time. (Wang et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the Linformer after explaining the quadratic cost of standard attention; use the right panel to motivate why linear approximations matter at scale.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel figure: (Left) Linformer architecture diagram showing linear projection layers E_i and F_i added to keys and values to reduce sequence dimension from L to k; (Right) Log-scale plot comparing inference time vs. sequence length for Linformer vs. standard Transformer, showing Linformer's linear scaling advantage.

image_metadata.caption: RFA reorders computation to avoid the full attention matrix. (Peng et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how kernel methods enable linear-time attention; use the side-by-side to make the reordering trick tangible before introducing the φ transformation formula.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel diagram comparing computation order: (Left) standard softmax attention computing the full L×L attention matrix before weighting values; (Right) Random Feature Attention (RFA) reordering operations to compute φ(Q)(φ(K)^T V) without materializing the full matrix, enabling linear complexity.

image_metadata.caption: Orthogonal and positive random features reduce approximation error in Performer. (Choromanski et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the RFA framework when discussing Performer's improvements; use to justify why orthogonal and positive feature choices are not arbitrary but empirically superior.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel plot comparing approximation error: (Left) i.i.d. random features vs. orthogonal random features showing orthogonal features have lower variance/error; (Right) sin/cos (signed) random features vs. positive random features showing positive features better approximate the softmax kernel.

image_metadata.caption: GTrXL adds gating units and reorders layer norm to stabilize RL training. (Parisotto et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing GTrXL after discussing why standard Transformers are hard to train in RL; use to walk through each modification and connect it to the stability motivation.
image_metadata.description: Three-column architecture diagram comparing: (1) Transformer-XL with standard layer norm placement; (2) Transformer-XL with reordered layer norm (pre-norm); (3) Gated Transformer-XL (GTrXL) replacing residual connections with gating units (GRU-style gates) and using reordered layer norm, enabling stable RL training.

image_metadata.caption: Bahdanau et al. (2014): attention mechanism for RNN-based machine translation.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce this when explaining the historical motivation for attention — before presenting the Transformer — to show how RNNs struggled with long-range dependencies and how attention addressed that.
image_metadata.description: Diagram from Bahdanau et al. (2014) illustrating the attention mechanism applied to a recurrent neural network for neural machine translation, showing how alignment scores connect encoder hidden states to decoder outputs.

image_metadata.caption: Vaswani et al. (2017): original Transformer encoder-decoder architecture diagram.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing the Transformer architecture; use as a reference diagram throughout discussions of attention, layer normalization placement, and encoder vs. decoder variants.
image_metadata.description: The original Transformer encoder-decoder architecture diagram from 'Attention Is All You Need' (Vaswani et al., 2017), showing stacked encoder and decoder blocks with multi-head attention, feed-forward layers, positional encoding, and residual connections.

image_metadata.caption: Post-LN vs. Pre-LN Transformer: layer normalization placement comparison (Vaswani 2017; Xiong 2020).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing layer normalization choices in Transformer design, especially when students ask why modern implementations differ from the original paper's figure.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of three Transformer sublayer configurations (1456×682): the original Post-LN Transformer from 'Attention Is All You Need' (left), an intermediate variant (center), and the Pre-LN Transformer from Xiong et al. (2020) (right), highlighting where layer normalization is placed relative to residual connections.

image_metadata.caption: Schmidhuber (1991): fast-weight programmer — an early precursor to Transformer self-attention.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Surface when discussing the theoretical foundations or history of attention mechanisms, particularly when covering linear Transformers or when students ask about the origins of self-attention.
image_metadata.description: Annotated diagram from Schmidhuber (1991) illustrating the fast-weight programmer concept, showing how a slow network generates weight updates for a fast network — an early precursor to the self-attention mechanism in Transformers.

image_metadata.caption: Howard & Ruder (2018): ULMFiT three-stage pretrain-then-finetune transfer learning pipeline.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the pretrain-finetune paradigm, before or alongside BERT and GPT discussions, to establish the conceptual recipe that all subsequent foundation models follow.
image_metadata.description: Diagram from Howard & Ruder (2018) ULMFiT paper (1456×702) illustrating the three-stage transfer learning pipeline: (1) language model pretraining on a large corpus, (2) language model fine-tuning on target data, and (3) classifier fine-tuning with gradual layer unfreezing.

image_metadata.caption: Devlin et al. (2018): BERT bidirectional encoder with masked language model pretraining.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the encoder-style LLM branch, directly contrasting with the GPT decoder-style diagram to highlight the architectural and objective differences.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram from Devlin et al. (2018) BERT paper, showing the bidirectional Transformer encoder with masked language modeling (MLM) and next sentence prediction (NSP) pretraining objectives, and how BERT embeddings are applied to downstream tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Radford & Narasimhan (2018): GPT decoder-only autoregressive Transformer architecture.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the BERT diagram when teaching the encoder vs. decoder split; emphasize that this architecture is the foundation of modern generative LLMs including ChatGPT.
image_metadata.description: Architecture and task diagram from the original GPT paper (Radford & Narasimhan, 2018), illustrating the decoder-only Transformer with unidirectional (left-to-right) autoregressive language modeling pretraining and task-specific fine-tuning heads.

image_metadata.caption: Lewis et al. (2019): BART denoising sequence-to-sequence pretraining architecture.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after covering BERT and GPT to illustrate how encoder-decoder models like BART synthesize both approaches; useful when discussing summarization, translation, and comprehension tasks.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram from Lewis et al. (2019) BART paper, showing the sequence-to-sequence denoising pretraining approach that combines a bidirectional encoder with an autoregressive decoder, along with various corruption/noising strategies applied to input text.

image_metadata.caption: Evolutionary tree of modern LLMs showing encoder, decoder, and hybrid lineages. (Yang et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of a lesson on LLM architectures to orient students before diving into specific model families; use it as a reference map when introducing any individual model.
image_metadata.description: An evolutionary tree (phylogenetic-style diagram) showing the lineage and branching of modern LLMs, distinguishing encoder-only (BERT-style), decoder-only (GPT-style), and encoder-decoder architectures over time.

image_metadata.caption: FlashAttention reduces memory bottlenecks via IO-aware tiled attention computation. (Dao et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when teaching attention mechanism optimizations or when students ask why standard attention doesn't scale well to long sequences.
image_metadata.description: A diagram or figure from the FlashAttention paper (Dao et al., 2022) illustrating the IO-aware attention mechanism, likely showing GPU memory hierarchy (HBM vs. SRAM) and how tiled computation reduces memory reads/writes.

image_metadata.caption: Training BERT on one GPU in 24h reveals size-efficiency trade-offs. (Geiping & Goldstein, 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when discussing practical constraints of LLM training or when introducing scaling laws; pairs well with Chinchilla scaling law discussion.
image_metadata.description: A figure from the 'Cramming' paper showing training dynamics or throughput trade-offs when training a BERT-style masked language model on a single GPU for 24 hours, likely comparing model size vs. training efficiency or performance thresholds.

image_metadata.caption: LoRA decomposes weight updates into low-rank matrices, enabling efficient fine-tuning. (Hu et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing fine-tuning strategies, especially after explaining why full fine-tuning of large models is computationally prohibitive.
image_metadata.description: A two-part illustration: (left) the LoRA mechanism showing how weight updates ΔW are decomposed into two low-rank matrices A and B added alongside frozen pretrained weights; (right) a performance comparison table or chart showing LoRA matches or exceeds full fine-tuning with far fewer trainable parameters.

image_metadata.caption: Taxonomy of parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods beyond LoRA. (Lialin et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing LoRA to broaden the student's view of fine-tuning options; useful for students who need to choose a PEFT method for a specific task.
image_metadata.description: A taxonomy or landscape diagram from the parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) survey, categorizing various PEFT methods (e.g., adapter layers, prefix tuning, LoRA, prompt tuning) and their relationships.

image_metadata.caption: Gopher's 280B model uses RMSNorm for improved training stability at scale. (Rae et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when teaching normalization layers in transformers or when discussing engineering decisions that matter at scale; good contrast point between theory and practice.
image_metadata.description: A figure from the Gopher paper showing architecture details or training analysis of the 280B parameter Gopher model, likely illustrating normalization choices (RMSNorm vs. LayerNorm) or training stability metrics across model scales.

image_metadata.caption: Chinchilla scaling laws show LLMs are undertrained; tokens matter as much as parameters. (Hoffmann et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing scaling laws; use to challenge the assumption that bigger models are always better and to motivate data-centric approaches to LLM training.
image_metadata.description: A figure from the Chinchilla paper showing compute-optimal scaling laws, likely a plot of model size vs. training tokens with iso-compute curves, demonstrating that optimal performance requires balancing model size and dataset size rather than maximizing model parameters alone.

image_metadata.caption: Pythia suite: 8 open LLMs from 70M–12B for studying training and scaling. (Biderman et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when discussing open-source LLM research infrastructure or when teaching about controlled experiments in ML; also relevant when discussing the effect of training data repetition.
image_metadata.description: An overview figure of the Pythia model suite showing the range of open-source models (70M to 12B parameters) trained on the same data with checkpoints saved throughout training, enabling controlled comparisons across scale and training duration.

image_metadata.caption: InstructGPT's 3-step RLHF pipeline: SFT, reward modeling, then PPO optimization. (Ouyang et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing alignment techniques or RLHF; essential visual for any lesson on how ChatGPT-style models are trained to follow instructions safely.
image_metadata.description: A three-step diagram from the InstructGPT paper illustrating the RLHF pipeline: (1) supervised fine-tuning on human-written prompt-response pairs, (2) training a reward model from human preference rankings, and (3) optimizing the language model with PPO using the reward model.

image_metadata.caption: Constitutional AI uses AI self-critique to train harmless models without human labels. (Bai et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after RLHF (Image 9) to contrast human feedback with AI-generated feedback; useful for discussions on scalable alignment and reducing reliance on human annotators.
image_metadata.description: A diagram from the Constitutional AI paper illustrating the CAI pipeline, likely showing how an AI model critiques and revises its own outputs according to a set of principles (the 'constitution'), replacing human feedback with AI-generated feedback for harmlessness training.

image_metadata.caption: Self-Instruct pipeline: bootstrapping instruction data with a pretrained LLM. (Raschka, adapted from Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing instruction-tuning alternatives to human-labeled datasets, specifically when explaining how models like Alpaca were trained cheaply at scale.
image_metadata.description: Annotated flowchart of the Self-Instruct pipeline showing four stages: (1) generating task instructions from a seed set using a pretrained LLM, (2) classifying task category, (3) generating input/output instances, and (4) filtering low-quality examples before adding to the instruction pool for fine-tuning.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer encoder–decoder architecture overview (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the Transformer at a high level, or when a student confuses encoder self-attention with decoder cross-attention; use it to trace one token’s path from input embedding to output probabilities.
image_metadata.description: Full Transformer encoder–decoder architecture diagram showing stacked encoder layers (self-attention + feed-forward with residual connections and layer normalization), stacked decoder layers (masked self-attention, encoder–decoder cross-attention, feed-forward), plus input/output embeddings, positional encodings, and final linear + softmax output.

image_metadata.caption: Scaled dot-product and multi-head attention schematic (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when deriving the attention formula or implementing attention; use it to explain tensor shapes (Q,K,V) and to justify multi-head attention vs a single attention head.
image_metadata.description: Figure 2 composite: left panel illustrates scaled dot-product attention as Q, K, V matrices multiplied (QK^T), scaled by 1/sqrt(d_k), passed through softmax to produce attention weights, then multiplied by V; right panel shows multi-head attention with multiple parallel attention heads whose outputs are concatenated and linearly projected.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head attention computation pipeline (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing scaled dot-product attention, when moving to multi-head attention implementation details (e.g., splitting/reshaping tensors and combining heads).
image_metadata.description: Larger rendering of Figure 2 emphasizing the multi-head attention block: separate linear projections create per-head Q, K, V; each head performs scaled dot-product attention; head outputs are concatenated and passed through a final linear layer.

image_metadata.caption: Encoder self-attention links distant dependency for “making” (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student asks how Transformers handle long-range context, or when contrasting self-attention with RNNs for dependency tracking; use it to discuss head diversity.
image_metadata.description: Attention visualization for the encoder self-attention (layer 5 of 6) focusing on the token “making”; colored lines/weights from “making” to other tokens show multiple heads attending strongly to a distant phrase completion (“making … more difficult”).

image_metadata.caption: Attention heads focusing on “its” for anaphora-like links (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching interpretability of attention heads or discussing whether attention can reflect linguistic structure; use it to explain how pronouns may attend to antecedents.
image_metadata.description: Anaphora-related attention visualization: top shows full attention pattern for a specific head (head 5) across tokens; bottom isolates attention originating from the word “its” for heads 5 and 6, showing very sharp, focused links to likely antecedents.

image_metadata.caption: Isolated attention from “its” across two heads (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the first anaphora figure when comparing multiple heads’ behavior on the same token; useful for exercises interpreting attention maps.
image_metadata.description: Second panel associated with Figure 4: additional attention heatmaps/links for heads 5 and 6, emphasizing isolated attention from the token “its” and the sharpness of the resulting attention peaks.

image_metadata.caption: Example head exhibiting sentence-structure-like attention (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing emergent linguistic structure in Transformers or when students need intuition for why multiple heads are useful beyond redundancy.
image_metadata.description: Sentence-structure-related attention visualization example 1 from encoder self-attention (layer 5 of 6), showing one head’s attention aligning tokens in a pattern consistent with syntactic structure (e.g., phrase boundaries or dependency-like links).

image_metadata.caption: Another head with distinct structure-related attention (Vaswani et al., 2017).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the other sentence-structure example to prompt comparison (What differs? What might each head be tracking?).
image_metadata.description: Sentence-structure-related attention visualization example 2 from a different encoder self-attention head (layer 5 of 6), contrasting with the previous example to show a different learned attention pattern/task.

lessons.title: Self-Driving & Autonomous Navigation
lessons.content:
# Self-Driving & Autonomous Navigation

Imagine driving through a busy intersection: pedestrians step off the curb, a cyclist appears from behind a parked van, the car ahead hesitates, and the light turns yellow. Humans handle this by constantly looping: **sense → interpret → anticipate → decide → act**. Autonomous driving systems try to build that same loop—reliably, at scale, and under messy real-world uncertainty.

## The autonomy stack: perception → prediction → planning → control
A useful mental model is a pipeline (even if modern systems sometimes blur the boundaries):

- **Perception**: turn raw sensors (cameras, radar, lidar) into a structured understanding—lanes, vehicles, pedestrians, free space.
- **Prediction**: estimate what other agents might do next (trajectories, intent, uncertainty).
- **Planning**: choose the ego vehicle’s future path and speed to be safe, legal, and comfortable.
- **Control**: convert the plan into steering, throttle, and brake commands that the vehicle can actually execute.

This decomposition matters because each stage has different failure modes. A perception glitch might “hallucinate” an obstacle; a planning bug might cut in too aggressively even with perfect perception.

## Why BEV is such a big deal
If you’ve ever tried to reason about traffic from a front-facing camera view, you know it’s awkward: perspective distortion, occlusions, and objects at different depths.

A **bird’s-eye view (BEV) representation** reprojects sensor information into a top-down grid around the car. In BEV, geometry becomes simpler:

- distances are in meters, not pixels
- lanes look like lanes (not converging lines)
- planning can operate in the same coordinate frame as maps and vehicle motion

BEV is less about “pretty pictures” and more about giving downstream modules a *workable coordinate system*.

## Occupancy networks: predicting the world as “what’s filled vs free”
Classic perception often outputs discrete objects: bounding boxes + classes. But driving also needs to understand *space*: drivable area, curbs, unknown regions, and partially observed zones.

An **occupancy network** predicts, for each cell (or voxel) in space, something like:

- **occupied** (something is there)
- **free** (safe to drive through)
- **unknown** (not observed / uncertain)

This is powerful because it naturally handles weird cases: debris, construction cones, a trailer with an unusual shape—anything that occupies space, even if it’s hard to classify.

## End-to-end learning vs modular systems
There are two broad philosophies:

1. **Modular**: train perception, prediction, planning, control as separate components with explicit interfaces (objects, lanes, occupancy, trajectories).
2. **End-to-end**: learn a direct mapping from sensors (and maybe map context) to actions or trajectories.

End-to-end can reduce hand-designed glue code, but it raises hard questions: *How do you debug it? How do you guarantee safety constraints? How do you know why it chose that maneuver?* In practice, many real systems are hybrids: learned perception + learned prediction + optimization-based planning, all wrapped in safety checks.

## Levels of autonomy (what “self-driving” actually means)
When people say “autonomous,” they often mean different things. **Levels of autonomy** separate driver assistance from true driverless operation. The key axis is: *who is responsible for monitoring the environment and handling failures—the human or the system?*

- lower levels: the human supervises continuously
- higher levels: the system can handle the full driving task within a defined operational domain

## Where platforms like NVIDIA DRIVE fit
A production AV stack isn’t just neural nets—it’s also real-time compute, sensor IO, redundancy, and deployment tooling. Platforms such as **NVIDIA DRIVE** are designed to run these workloads (multi-camera perception, BEV fusion, occupancy, planning) under strict latency and safety constraints.

## A tiny “planning from occupancy” sketch
Below is a toy example: treat occupancy as a grid costmap and pick the lowest-cost next step. Real planners are far more sophisticated, but the idea—**plan in a spatial representation**—is the same.

```python
import numpy as np

# 0 = free, 1 = occupied
occ = np.zeros((10, 10), dtype=int)
occ[4:7, 6] = 1  # a vertical obstacle

start = (9, 1)
goal  = (0, 8)

def neighbors(r, c):
    for dr, dc in [(-1,0),(1,0),(0,-1),(0,1)]:
        rr, cc = r+dr, c+dc
        if 0 <= rr < 10 and 0 <= cc < 10 and occ[rr, cc] == 0:
            yield (rr, cc)

# Greedy step: move to neighbor that reduces Manhattan distance
pos = start
path = [pos]
for _ in range(50):
    if pos == goal:
        break
    r, c = pos
    cand = list(neighbors(r, c))
    if not cand:
        break
    pos = min(cand, key=lambda p: abs(p[0]-goal[0]) + abs(p[1]-goal[1]))
    path.append(pos)

print(path)
```

## Summary
Autonomous navigation is a closed loop: perceive the scene, predict how it evolves, plan a safe trajectory, and control the vehicle to follow it. BEV and occupancy networks are popular because they turn messy sensor data into spatial representations that make planning and safety reasoning much more natural.

## Recommended Reading
- [Perception, Planning, and Control (MIT Manipulation notes)](https://manipulation.csail.mit.edu/intro.html) — A clear, systems-level view of how these pieces fit together in robotics.
- [Part 1: Key Concepts in RL (Spinning Up)](https://spinningup.openai.com/en/latest/spinningup/rl_intro.html) — Helpful background if you’re thinking about learning-based driving policies.
- [A (Long) Peek into Reinforcement Learning](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2018-02-19-rl-overview/) — A practitioner-friendly foundation for decision-making under uncertainty.
lessons.summary: Autonomous driving systems combine perception, prediction, planning, and control to navigate dynamic traffic scenes. BEV and occupancy-based representations make the world easier to reason about geometrically, enabling safer and more robust planning on real-time platforms.
lessons.video_title: Deep Reinforcement Learning" (CS285 Lecture 1 / intro lecture)

image_metadata.caption: Agent-environment interaction loop: state, action, reward cycle. (Lilian Weng)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RL introduction, before defining formal terms, to ground the intuition of the learning loop.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing the agent-environment interaction loop: an agent observes state and reward from the environment, then takes an action, which feeds back into the environment.

image_metadata.caption: RL algorithm taxonomy: model-based vs. model-free, value vs. policy. (David Silver)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing basic RL concepts and before diving into specific algorithms, as a roadmap students can return to throughout the course.
image_metadata.description: A taxonomy diagram categorizing RL algorithms into model-based vs model-free, and within model-free into value-based, policy-based, and actor-critic approaches, with example algorithms placed in each category.

image_metadata.caption: MDP agent-environment interaction with timestep notation. (Sutton & Barto 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing MDPs formally, after students understand the intuitive loop, to connect intuition to the mathematical framework.
image_metadata.description: The classic Sutton & Barto diagram of the MDP agent-environment interaction cycle, showing the agent receiving state S_t and reward R_t at each timestep and outputting action A_t.

image_metadata.caption: Work-day MDP example: states, actions, and rewards illustrated. (randomant.net)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the formal MDP definition to provide an accessible worked example before moving to mathematical derivations.
image_metadata.description: A fun illustrative MDP example using a typical work day: states include 'home', 'office', 'lunch', etc., with actions and transitions shown as arrows, and rewards annotated on transitions.

image_metadata.caption: Bellman backup diagrams for V(s) and Q(s,a) value functions. (Lilian Weng)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when deriving Bellman equations, using the diagram to trace through the one-step lookahead and explain the expectation over next states.
image_metadata.description: Backup diagrams illustrating Bellman expectation equations for both state-value V(s) and action-value Q(s,a) functions, showing how current values are computed from successor states and rewards.

image_metadata.caption: Backup diagrams contrasting Q-learning and SARSA update targets. (Sutton & Barto 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing TD control methods to visually contrast how SARSA and Q-learning differ in their update targets before presenting the pseudocode.
image_metadata.description: Backup diagrams comparing Q-learning (off-policy) and SARSA (on-policy) TD control, showing the difference in which next action is used for the update target.

image_metadata.caption: Q-learning (off-policy) vs. SARSA (on-policy) backup diagrams compared. (Sutton & Barto 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show side-by-side when students struggle to articulate the difference between SARSA and Q-learning, pointing to the branching structure to explain the update rule.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side backup diagrams for Q-learning and SARSA, explicitly labeling the greedy max selection in Q-learning versus the policy-sampled next action in SARSA.

image_metadata.caption: DQN algorithm with experience replay and frozen target network. (Mnih et al. 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching DQN after explaining why naive Q-learning with neural networks is unstable, using each step of the pseudocode to explain how replay and frozen targets address instability.
image_metadata.description: The full DQN algorithm pseudocode from Mnih et al. 2015, including experience replay buffer initialization, epsilon-greedy action selection, minibatch sampling, and periodic frozen target network updates.

image_metadata.caption: TD(λ) backup depth interpolates between TD and Monte-Carlo methods. (David Silver)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing TD(λ) after students understand both 1-step TD and MC, to illustrate how λ interpolates between the two extremes.
image_metadata.description: A diagram comparing backup depth across TD(λ), showing how λ-return interpolates between one-step TD (shallow backup) and full Monte-Carlo (deep backup) using exponentially decaying weights.

image_metadata.caption: MC, TD, and DP backup diagrams: depth and width compared. (David Silver)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a synthesis diagram after covering all three methods, or use it as a preview at the start to give students a conceptual map of where each method sits.
image_metadata.description: Three backup diagrams side by side comparing Monte-Carlo (full episode depth), TD learning (one-step depth), and Dynamic Programming (full-width one-step), illustrating the depth and width of each method's update.

image_metadata.caption: Parallel ES-RL: workers share seeds to reconstruct noise cheaply. (Salimans et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Evolution Strategies as an alternative to MDP/TD-based RL, specifically when explaining how ES scales efficiently via parallelism and why shared random seeds reduce communication cost.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart showing a parallel Evolution Strategies RL algorithm where a master node distributes random seeds to multiple parallel workers, each worker evaluates a perturbed policy in the environment, and returns scalar rewards back to the master for parameter updates. Workers share random seeds to reconstruct Gaussian noise without transmitting full gradient vectors.

image_metadata.caption: 19×19 Go board: liberties and eyes define stone survival rules.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the AlphaGo Zero case study, before discussing the neural network architecture or MCTS, to ensure students understand the environment the RL agent operates in.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of a 19x19 Go board showing the placement of black and white stones, illustrating key game concepts: liberties (open intersection points adjacent to a stone group), eyes (enclosed liberties needed for a group to stay alive), and the positional superko rule.

image_metadata.caption: AlphaGo Zero self-play loop: MCTS refines policy each step. (Silver et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how AlphaGo Zero trains purely from self-play, especially to contrast with supervised learning from human expert data used in the original AlphaGo, and to illustrate how MCTS and neural networks interact iteratively.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing the AlphaGo Zero self-play training loop: the current neural network policy plays games against itself via MCTS, generating training data (board states, MCTS policy targets, game outcomes), which are used to update the network weights, and the improved network is then used for the next round of self-play.

image_metadata.caption: General policy gradient form; Ψ varies by algorithm. (Schulman et al., 2016)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce this immediately after deriving the policy gradient theorem, before discussing specific algorithms, to anchor all subsequent methods to a common formula
image_metadata.description: Mathematical expression showing the general form of the policy gradient, with expectation over state-action distributions under policy π, combining log probability of action and a function Ψ representing various return estimators

image_metadata.caption: A3C uses async updates; A2C synchronizes all actors before updating.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from A3C to A2C to highlight the single architectural change (coordinator/synchronization) that distinguishes the two methods
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side architectural diagram comparing A3C (asynchronous) and A2C (synchronous) showing multiple parallel actors, their interaction with a global network, and the role of a coordinator in A2C that synchronizes gradient updates before applying them

image_metadata.caption: DDPG algorithm with soft target updates and exploration noise. (Lillicrap et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Present after explaining the motivation for DDPG (continuous actions, off-policy learning), so students can map each algorithmic step to the conceptual components already discussed
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for the DDPG algorithm detailing initialization of critic and actor networks with random weights, replay buffer setup, soft target network updates, exploration noise addition, and the actor-critic update steps per episode

image_metadata.caption: D4PG extends DDPG with distributional critic and N parallel actors. (Barth-Maron et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after DDPG algorithm is understood; use to walk through each of the three D4PG improvements one by one, pointing to the corresponding pseudocode lines
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for the D4PG algorithm showing distributed actors feeding a shared replay buffer, distributional critic updates using N-step returns, and priority-weighted sampling, extending DDPG with distributional and distributed components

image_metadata.caption: MADDPG: centralized critics, decentralized actors per agent. (Lowe et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing multi-agent RL challenges; use the diagram to explain why a centralized critic stabilizes training even when agents act independently at test time
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of MADDPG showing multiple agents each with their own actor (using local observations) and a centralized critic (using global observations and all agents' actions), illustrating the centralized training with decentralized execution paradigm

image_metadata.caption: PPG alternates policy and auxiliary phases to decouple training. (Cobbe et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Present after explaining the parameter-sharing trade-off in actor-critic methods; use the two-phase structure to show how PPG resolves the conflict between policy and value objectives
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for the PPG (Phasic Policy Gradient) algorithm showing two alternating training phases: a policy phase updating the policy network with PPO, and an auxiliary phase updating the value network with distillation loss to prevent policy drift

image_metadata.caption: PPG outperforms PPO in sample efficiency on Procgen. (Cobbe et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the PPG algorithm diagram to validate the claim that phase separation improves sample efficiency; prompt students to identify which environments benefit most
image_metadata.description: Bar chart or learning curve comparing mean normalized performance of PPG versus PPO across multiple Procgen benchmark environments, showing PPG achieving higher sample efficiency

image_metadata.caption: SAC algorithm with entropy regularization and fixed temperature α. (Haarnoja et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce after explaining the maximum entropy RL objective; walk through each update step to show how the entropy term modifies standard actor-critic updates
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm showing entropy-regularized policy optimization with soft Q-function updates, policy update via reparameterization, and fixed temperature parameter α balancing reward and entropy

image_metadata.caption: SAC with auto-tuned temperature α via dual gradient descent. (Haarnoja et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the fixed-temperature SAC algorithm; highlight the single added line updating α and explain why this resolves the brittleness of manual temperature selection across different tasks
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for SAC with automatically adjusted temperature, adding a gradient-based update rule for α that minimizes the difference between current entropy and a target entropy threshold, removing the need for manual temperature tuning

image_metadata.caption: TD3 algorithm pseudocode showing twin critics, delayed updates, and policy smoothing. (Fujimoto et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining each of TD3's three core improvements (clipped double Q-learning, delayed updates, target policy smoothing) so students can see how all three are integrated into a single coherent algorithm.
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode/algorithm box for TD3 (Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient), detailing initialization, actor-critic network setup, replay buffer, and the training loop including target policy smoothing with clipped noise, twin critic updates, and delayed actor/target network updates.

image_metadata.caption: IMPALA architecture: parallel actors feed a central learner with off-policy correction.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing IMPALA's motivation for scalability, before diving into V-trace math, so students first grasp the high-level system design and understand why the behavior and training policies diverge.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of IMPALA showing multiple actor machines generating experience trajectories in parallel and sending them to a central learner, which updates policy and value function parameters and periodically broadcasts updated weights back to actors.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo.py vs. OpenAI Baselines PPO: average episodic returns over training steps.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching students how to validate a new RL implementation — use it to discuss the importance of reproducing baseline results before claiming novelty.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns over training steps for CleanRL's ppo.py compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO across multiple classic control or MuJoCo environments, plotted against environment steps.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo.py vs. OpenAI Baselines PPO: episodic returns vs. wall-clock time.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the distinction between sample efficiency and time efficiency, or when students ask why wall-clock time matters in addition to environment steps.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns for CleanRL's ppo.py plotted against wall-clock time (rather than environment steps), compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo_atari.py vs. OpenAI Baselines PPO on Atari: average episodic returns.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing PPO on image-based environments or when contrasting low-dimensional state spaces (classic control) with high-dimensional pixel inputs (Atari).
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns over training steps for CleanRL's ppo_atari.py compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO on Atari game environments.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo_atari.py vs. OpenAI Baselines on Atari: episodic returns vs. wall-clock time.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the computational overhead of convolutional networks in RL, or when comparing training time across different environment types.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns for CleanRL's ppo_atari.py plotted against wall-clock time, compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO on Atari environments.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo_continuous_action.py on dm_control: average episodic returns over training steps.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing PPO for continuous control tasks, or when contrasting discrete-action (Atari) and continuous-action (dm_control/MuJoCo) RL settings.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns over training steps for CleanRL's ppo_continuous_action.py across DeepMind Control Suite (dm_control) continuous-action environments.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo_continuous_action.py on dm_control: episodic returns vs. wall-clock time.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the steps-based chart (Image 5) to teach students how to interpret both sample efficiency and time efficiency for continuous control.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns for CleanRL's ppo_continuous_action.py plotted against wall-clock time across dm_control continuous-action environments.

image_metadata.caption: PPO on Gymnasium dm_control environments; note LQR tasks never terminate or truncate.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing environment wrappers, episode boundaries, or when students encounter environments that never terminate — use to highlight the importance of reading environment documentation.
