image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the concept of self-reflection; use the diagram to walk through each stage of the Reflexion loop step by step.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart illustrating the Reflexion framework, showing the cycle of: Actor generating actions, Evaluator scoring trajectories, Self-Reflection module producing verbal feedback, and that feedback being stored in episodic memory to inform the next trial.

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion outperforms baselines; hallucination dominates AlfWorld failures. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the Reflexion framework to ground the theory in experimental results; use to discuss the relative prevalence of hallucination vs. planning failures.
image_metadata.description: Bar charts comparing Reflexion against baselines on AlfWorld and HotpotQA benchmarks, broken down by failure type (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), showing performance across successive trials.

image_metadata.caption: CoH fine-tuning: model learns from ranked, annotated output sequences. (Liu et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when teaching CoH as a self-reflection mechanism; use to contrast with RLHF and highlight how explicit feedback sequences differ from scalar reward signals.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing how Chain of Hindsight (CoH) fine-tuning works: a sequence of model outputs annotated with feedback labels (Bad, Good, Better, Best) is fed as input, and the model learns to produce progressively improved outputs conditioned on that sequence.

image_metadata.caption: Algorithm Distillation distills RL learning histories into a neural network. (Laskin et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when introducing in-context RL and AD; the diagram helps students grasp why multi-episode context is necessary and how the distillation process works.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating Algorithm Distillation (AD): source RL policies generate multi-episode learning histories per task, which are concatenated and used to train a single transformer via behavioral cloning to perform in-context RL.

image_metadata.caption: AD matches RL² on memory/exploration tasks with binary reward only. (Laskin et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining AD to substantiate its claims; prompt students to compare AD vs. RL² and discuss what the results imply about context length requirements.
image_metadata.description: Performance comparison charts of AD, Expert Distillation (ED), source policy, and RL² on memory-and-exploration environments (dark rooms, watermaze), showing AD matches or exceeds RL² with binary rewards only.

image_metadata.caption: Human memory taxonomy mapped to LLM agent memory components. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce at the start of the memory module; use the biological analogy to motivate design choices for agent memory systems before discussing technical implementations.
image_metadata.description: A hierarchical diagram categorizing human memory into Sensory Memory (iconic, echoic, haptic), Short-Term/Working Memory, and Long-Term Memory (explicit/declarative and implicit/procedural), with mappings to LLM agent memory equivalents.

image_metadata.caption: MIPS algorithm comparison: recall@10 vs. query speed tradeoff. (Google Blog, 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing external memory retrieval mechanisms; use to motivate the choice of ANN over exact search and to compare specific algorithm tradeoffs.
image_metadata.description: A recall@10 performance comparison chart of multiple Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) / MIPS algorithms (e.g., HNSW, FAISS, ScaNN) plotted against query speed, showing the accuracy-speed tradeoff.

image_metadata.caption: HuggingGPT's four-stage pipeline: LLM plans, selects, and coordinates AI models. (Shen et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the 'Tool Use' component of LLM agents, specifically to demonstrate how a real system (HuggingGPT) chains task planning → model selection → execution → response, reinforcing the four-stage pipeline described in the text.
image_metadata.description: A multi-stage pipeline diagram showing HuggingGPT's architecture: user request flows through task planning, model selection, task execution, and response generation stages, with the LLM acting as a central controller coordinating specialized AI models from HuggingFace.

image_metadata.caption: API-Bank pseudo-code showing LLM decision logic for tool selection and API calls. (Li et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Surface this after explaining HuggingGPT to deepen understanding of how tool-use accuracy is measured; use it when discussing how benchmarks decompose agent capability into discrete, testable decision points.
image_metadata.description: Pseudo-code block illustrating the decision logic an LLM follows when making API calls in the API-Bank benchmark, showing conditional branching for API selection, parameter filling, and call execution with evaluation checkpoints at each step.

image_metadata.caption: Generative agent architecture: memory stream, retrieval, reflection, and planning loop. (Park et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when teaching memory and reflection components of LLM agents, or when discussing multi-agent simulations; particularly effective for showing how long-term memory and social interaction emerge from combining core agent components.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the Generative Agents system showing how a single LLM-powered agent processes observations through a memory stream, retrieves relevant memories via a retrieval module, reflects to form higher-level insights, and plans/executes actions in a sandbox environment with 25 interacting virtual characters.

image_metadata.caption: Three approaches to sim2real transfer: system ID, domain randomization, and domain adaptation.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on sim2real transfer to orient students to the landscape of approaches before explaining any single method in depth.
image_metadata.description: A conceptual diagram showing three parallel approaches to sim2real transfer: system identification, domain randomization, and domain adaptation, each illustrated as a pathway from simulator to real world.

image_metadata.caption: Randomized training images from Tobin et al. (2017) illustrating visual domain randomization.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing uniform domain randomization, especially when explaining why randomizing visual parameters helps policies generalize to unseen real-world appearances.
image_metadata.description: A grid of rendered images showing the same robotic manipulation scene with varied visual properties — different textures, lighting, colors, and backgrounds — demonstrating uniform domain randomization applied to training environments.

image_metadata.caption: Learning to Simulate: task feedback optimizes simulator parameters φ. (Ruiz, 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from uniform DR to optimization-based DR, to help students see how feedback from the target domain can replace hand-tuned randomization ranges.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart-style overview of the 'Learning to Simulate' framework, showing how a simulator parameterized by φ generates training data, a policy is trained on it, the policy is evaluated on a target task, and feedback is used to optimize the simulation parameters.

image_metadata.caption: SimOpt iteratively aligns simulator and real-world trajectory distributions. (Chebotar et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining methods that use real-world data to guide DR, contrasting it with purely simulation-based approaches like uniform DR or Learning to Simulate.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of the SimOpt framework showing an iterative loop: initial randomization distribution → policy training in sim → deployment on both sim and real robot → trajectory comparison → optimization of randomization distribution parameters.

image_metadata.caption: RCAN translates randomized or real images to canonical simulator views. (James et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing image-based domain adaptation methods, particularly to contrast the 'translate to canonical sim' strategy with the 'randomize sim to cover real' strategy of DR.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing RCAN's image-conditional GAN architecture: it takes a domain-randomized or real image as input and outputs a canonical non-randomized simulator image, which is then used for policy training.

image_metadata.caption: DeceptionNet uses adversarial deception modules to generate harder training images. (Zakharov et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when contrasting different objectives for guided DR — specifically to highlight the 'maximize difficulty' objective versus the 'maximize task performance' objective used in other methods.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of the DeceptionNet architecture showing deception modules (encoder-decoder networks) that transform input images with various randomizations, feeding into a downstream recognition/classification network whose loss signal trains the deception modules adversarially.

image_metadata.caption: ADR adaptively expands randomization bounds using trajectory similarity feedback. (Mehta et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining adaptive or curriculum-based domain randomization, to illustrate how the range of randomization parameters can be dynamically adjusted based on policy performance signals.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart of Active Domain Randomization (ADR) showing how a discriminator compares sim and real trajectories to measure environment similarity, which drives adaptive expansion or contraction of randomization parameter bounds during training.

image_metadata.caption: Agent-environment interaction loop: state, action, reward cycle. (Lilian Weng)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RL introduction, before defining formal terms, to ground the intuition of the learning loop.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing the agent-environment interaction loop: an agent observes state and reward from the environment, then takes an action, which feeds back into the environment.

image_metadata.caption: RL algorithm taxonomy: model-based vs. model-free, value vs. policy. (David Silver)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing basic RL concepts and before diving into specific algorithms, as a roadmap students can return to throughout the course.
image_metadata.description: A taxonomy diagram categorizing RL algorithms into model-based vs model-free, and within model-free into value-based, policy-based, and actor-critic approaches, with example algorithms placed in each category.

image_metadata.caption: MDP agent-environment interaction with timestep notation. (Sutton & Barto 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing MDPs formally, after students understand the intuitive loop, to connect intuition to the mathematical framework.
image_metadata.description: The classic Sutton & Barto diagram of the MDP agent-environment interaction cycle, showing the agent receiving state S_t and reward R_t at each timestep and outputting action A_t.

image_metadata.caption: Work-day MDP example: states, actions, and rewards illustrated. (randomant.net)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the formal MDP definition to provide an accessible worked example before moving to mathematical derivations.
image_metadata.description: A fun illustrative MDP example using a typical work day: states include 'home', 'office', 'lunch', etc., with actions and transitions shown as arrows, and rewards annotated on transitions.

image_metadata.caption: Bellman backup diagrams for V(s) and Q(s,a) value functions. (Lilian Weng)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when deriving Bellman equations, using the diagram to trace through the one-step lookahead and explain the expectation over next states.
image_metadata.description: Backup diagrams illustrating Bellman expectation equations for both state-value V(s) and action-value Q(s,a) functions, showing how current values are computed from successor states and rewards.

image_metadata.caption: Backup diagrams contrasting Q-learning and SARSA update targets. (Sutton & Barto 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing TD control methods to visually contrast how SARSA and Q-learning differ in their update targets before presenting the pseudocode.
image_metadata.description: Backup diagrams comparing Q-learning (off-policy) and SARSA (on-policy) TD control, showing the difference in which next action is used for the update target.

image_metadata.caption: Q-learning (off-policy) vs. SARSA (on-policy) backup diagrams compared. (Sutton & Barto 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show side-by-side when students struggle to articulate the difference between SARSA and Q-learning, pointing to the branching structure to explain the update rule.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side backup diagrams for Q-learning and SARSA, explicitly labeling the greedy max selection in Q-learning versus the policy-sampled next action in SARSA.

image_metadata.caption: DQN algorithm with experience replay and frozen target network. (Mnih et al. 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching DQN after explaining why naive Q-learning with neural networks is unstable, using each step of the pseudocode to explain how replay and frozen targets address instability.
image_metadata.description: The full DQN algorithm pseudocode from Mnih et al. 2015, including experience replay buffer initialization, epsilon-greedy action selection, minibatch sampling, and periodic frozen target network updates.

image_metadata.caption: TD(λ) backup depth interpolates between TD and Monte-Carlo methods. (David Silver)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing TD(λ) after students understand both 1-step TD and MC, to illustrate how λ interpolates between the two extremes.
image_metadata.description: A diagram comparing backup depth across TD(λ), showing how λ-return interpolates between one-step TD (shallow backup) and full Monte-Carlo (deep backup) using exponentially decaying weights.

image_metadata.caption: MC, TD, and DP backup diagrams: depth and width compared. (David Silver)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a synthesis diagram after covering all three methods, or use it as a preview at the start to give students a conceptual map of where each method sits.
image_metadata.description: Three backup diagrams side by side comparing Monte-Carlo (full episode depth), TD learning (one-step depth), and Dynamic Programming (full-width one-step), illustrating the depth and width of each method's update.

image_metadata.caption: Parallel ES-RL: workers share seeds to reconstruct noise cheaply. (Salimans et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Evolution Strategies as an alternative to MDP/TD-based RL, specifically when explaining how ES scales efficiently via parallelism and why shared random seeds reduce communication cost.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart showing a parallel Evolution Strategies RL algorithm where a master node distributes random seeds to multiple parallel workers, each worker evaluates a perturbed policy in the environment, and returns scalar rewards back to the master for parameter updates. Workers share random seeds to reconstruct Gaussian noise without transmitting full gradient vectors.

image_metadata.caption: 19×19 Go board: liberties and eyes define stone survival rules.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the AlphaGo Zero case study, before discussing the neural network architecture or MCTS, to ensure students understand the environment the RL agent operates in.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of a 19x19 Go board showing the placement of black and white stones, illustrating key game concepts: liberties (open intersection points adjacent to a stone group), eyes (enclosed liberties needed for a group to stay alive), and the positional superko rule.

image_metadata.caption: AlphaGo Zero self-play loop: MCTS refines policy each step. (Silver et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how AlphaGo Zero trains purely from self-play, especially to contrast with supervised learning from human expert data used in the original AlphaGo, and to illustrate how MCTS and neural networks interact iteratively.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing the AlphaGo Zero self-play training loop: the current neural network policy plays games against itself via MCTS, generating training data (board states, MCTS policy targets, game outcomes), which are used to update the network weights, and the improved network is then used for the next round of self-play.

image_metadata.caption: General policy gradient form; Ψ varies by algorithm. (Schulman et al., 2016)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce this immediately after deriving the policy gradient theorem, before discussing specific algorithms, to anchor all subsequent methods to a common formula
image_metadata.description: Mathematical expression showing the general form of the policy gradient, with expectation over state-action distributions under policy π, combining log probability of action and a function Ψ representing various return estimators

image_metadata.caption: A3C uses async updates; A2C synchronizes all actors before updating.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from A3C to A2C to highlight the single architectural change (coordinator/synchronization) that distinguishes the two methods
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side architectural diagram comparing A3C (asynchronous) and A2C (synchronous) showing multiple parallel actors, their interaction with a global network, and the role of a coordinator in A2C that synchronizes gradient updates before applying them

image_metadata.caption: DDPG algorithm with soft target updates and exploration noise. (Lillicrap et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Present after explaining the motivation for DDPG (continuous actions, off-policy learning), so students can map each algorithmic step to the conceptual components already discussed
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for the DDPG algorithm detailing initialization of critic and actor networks with random weights, replay buffer setup, soft target network updates, exploration noise addition, and the actor-critic update steps per episode

image_metadata.caption: D4PG extends DDPG with distributional critic and N parallel actors. (Barth-Maron et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after DDPG algorithm is understood; use to walk through each of the three D4PG improvements one by one, pointing to the corresponding pseudocode lines
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for the D4PG algorithm showing distributed actors feeding a shared replay buffer, distributional critic updates using N-step returns, and priority-weighted sampling, extending DDPG with distributional and distributed components

image_metadata.caption: MADDPG: centralized critics, decentralized actors per agent. (Lowe et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing multi-agent RL challenges; use the diagram to explain why a centralized critic stabilizes training even when agents act independently at test time
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of MADDPG showing multiple agents each with their own actor (using local observations) and a centralized critic (using global observations and all agents' actions), illustrating the centralized training with decentralized execution paradigm

image_metadata.caption: PPG alternates policy and auxiliary phases to decouple training. (Cobbe et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Present after explaining the parameter-sharing trade-off in actor-critic methods; use the two-phase structure to show how PPG resolves the conflict between policy and value objectives
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for the PPG (Phasic Policy Gradient) algorithm showing two alternating training phases: a policy phase updating the policy network with PPO, and an auxiliary phase updating the value network with distillation loss to prevent policy drift

image_metadata.caption: PPG outperforms PPO in sample efficiency on Procgen. (Cobbe et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the PPG algorithm diagram to validate the claim that phase separation improves sample efficiency; prompt students to identify which environments benefit most
image_metadata.description: Bar chart or learning curve comparing mean normalized performance of PPG versus PPO across multiple Procgen benchmark environments, showing PPG achieving higher sample efficiency

image_metadata.caption: SAC algorithm with entropy regularization and fixed temperature α. (Haarnoja et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce after explaining the maximum entropy RL objective; walk through each update step to show how the entropy term modifies standard actor-critic updates
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm showing entropy-regularized policy optimization with soft Q-function updates, policy update via reparameterization, and fixed temperature parameter α balancing reward and entropy

image_metadata.caption: SAC with auto-tuned temperature α via dual gradient descent. (Haarnoja et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the fixed-temperature SAC algorithm; highlight the single added line updating α and explain why this resolves the brittleness of manual temperature selection across different tasks
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for SAC with automatically adjusted temperature, adding a gradient-based update rule for α that minimizes the difference between current entropy and a target entropy threshold, removing the need for manual temperature tuning

image_metadata.caption: TD3 algorithm pseudocode showing twin critics, delayed updates, and policy smoothing. (Fujimoto et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining each of TD3's three core improvements (clipped double Q-learning, delayed updates, target policy smoothing) so students can see how all three are integrated into a single coherent algorithm.
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode/algorithm box for TD3 (Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient), detailing initialization, actor-critic network setup, replay buffer, and the training loop including target policy smoothing with clipped noise, twin critic updates, and delayed actor/target network updates.

image_metadata.caption: IMPALA architecture: parallel actors feed a central learner with off-policy correction.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing IMPALA's motivation for scalability, before diving into V-trace math, so students first grasp the high-level system design and understand why the behavior and training policies diverge.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of IMPALA showing multiple actor machines generating experience trajectories in parallel and sending them to a central learner, which updates policy and value function parameters and periodically broadcasts updated weights back to actors.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo.py vs. OpenAI Baselines PPO: average episodic returns over training steps.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching students how to validate a new RL implementation — use it to discuss the importance of reproducing baseline results before claiming novelty.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns over training steps for CleanRL's ppo.py compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO across multiple classic control or MuJoCo environments, plotted against environment steps.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo.py vs. OpenAI Baselines PPO: episodic returns vs. wall-clock time.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the distinction between sample efficiency and time efficiency, or when students ask why wall-clock time matters in addition to environment steps.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns for CleanRL's ppo.py plotted against wall-clock time (rather than environment steps), compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo_atari.py vs. OpenAI Baselines PPO on Atari: average episodic returns.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing PPO on image-based environments or when contrasting low-dimensional state spaces (classic control) with high-dimensional pixel inputs (Atari).
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns over training steps for CleanRL's ppo_atari.py compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO on Atari game environments.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo_atari.py vs. OpenAI Baselines on Atari: episodic returns vs. wall-clock time.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the computational overhead of convolutional networks in RL, or when comparing training time across different environment types.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns for CleanRL's ppo_atari.py plotted against wall-clock time, compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO on Atari environments.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo_continuous_action.py on dm_control: average episodic returns over training steps.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing PPO for continuous control tasks, or when contrasting discrete-action (Atari) and continuous-action (dm_control/MuJoCo) RL settings.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns over training steps for CleanRL's ppo_continuous_action.py across DeepMind Control Suite (dm_control) continuous-action environments.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo_continuous_action.py on dm_control: episodic returns vs. wall-clock time.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the steps-based chart (Image 5) to teach students how to interpret both sample efficiency and time efficiency for continuous control.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns for CleanRL's ppo_continuous_action.py plotted against wall-clock time across dm_control continuous-action environments.

image_metadata.caption: PPO on Gymnasium dm_control environments; note LQR tasks never terminate or truncate.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing environment wrappers, episode boundaries, or when students encounter environments that never terminate — use to highlight the importance of reading environment documentation.
image_metadata.description: Performance chart for ppo_continuous_action.py on Gymnasium-wrapped dm_control environments, including special note about lqr environments that are never terminated or truncated, comparing results across multiple tasks.

image_metadata.caption: MuJoCo v2 vs. v4 environments: ppo_continuous_action.py performance is roughly equivalent.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing reproducibility in RL or when students ask about differences between gym and gymnasium, or between MuJoCo v2 and v4 environments.
image_metadata.description: Comparison chart showing average episodic returns for ppo_continuous_action.py using MuJoCo v2 environments versus v4 environments in Gymnasium, demonstrating performance equivalence across versions.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo_atari_lstm.py vs. OpenAI Baselines PPO on Atari: episodic returns with LSTM.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing recurrent policies in RL, or when discussing how memory can help agents in partially observable or temporally dependent tasks.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns over training steps for CleanRL's ppo_atari_lstm.py (PPO with LSTM recurrent policy) compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO on Atari environments.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo_atari_lstm.py vs. OpenAI Baselines on Atari: episodic returns vs. wall-clock time.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside Image 9 to contrast sample efficiency with time efficiency for recurrent PPO, or when discussing the overhead of backpropagation through time (BPTT).
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns for CleanRL's ppo_atari_lstm.py plotted against wall-clock time, compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO on Atari environments.

image_metadata.caption: PPO with EnvPool: episodic return vs. environment steps across Atari games.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing PPO's empirical performance on Atari games and the impact of using EnvPool for faster environment simulation
image_metadata.description: Learning curves (episodic return vs. environment steps) for PPO with Atari EnvPool across multiple Atari games, showing performance benchmarks

image_metadata.caption: PPO with EnvPool: episodic return vs. wall-clock time across Atari games.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When contrasting sample efficiency vs. computational efficiency, or when explaining the practical speedup from vectorized environment libraries like EnvPool
image_metadata.description: Learning curves (episodic return vs. wall-clock time) for PPO with Atari EnvPool, showing training speed rather than sample efficiency

image_metadata.caption: HNS comparison: PPO with vs. without value function clipping shows no significant difference.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching PPO implementation choices, specifically when discussing whether value function clipping is a necessary component of the algorithm
image_metadata.description: Bar chart or comparison plot of Human Normalized Score (HNS) for PPO vs. baseline implementations, likely comparing value function clipping variants

image_metadata.caption: PPO JAX/XLA+EnvPool sample and wall-clock efficiency vs. baselines across Atari.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining how hardware-accelerated RL frameworks (JAX+XLA) compare to standard implementations in terms of real-world training speed
image_metadata.description: Plot comparing sample efficiency and wall-clock time efficiency of PPO (XLA/JAX with EnvPool) against other implementations, possibly including SEEDRL R2D2

image_metadata.caption: PPO (JAX/XLA + EnvPool): episodic return vs. environment steps on Atari.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When comparing different PPO implementations or discussing the effect of JAX/XLA on learning performance (not just speed)
image_metadata.description: Learning curves (episodic return vs. environment steps) for PPO implemented with JAX/XLA and EnvPool across Atari games, benchmarked against baselines

image_metadata.caption: PPO (JAX/XLA + EnvPool): episodic return vs. wall-clock time on Atari.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the practical training time advantages of JAX/XLA-based PPO over standard PyTorch implementations
image_metadata.description: Learning curves (episodic return vs. wall-clock time) for PPO with JAX/XLA and EnvPool, showing real-time training speed across Atari games

image_metadata.caption: Median HNS: CleanRL PPO (JAX/XLA) vs. SEEDRL R2D2 across 57 Atari games.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing how PPO compares to state-of-the-art off-policy algorithms at scale, or when introducing the HNS metric for aggregate Atari evaluation
image_metadata.description: Median Human Normalized Score (HNS) comparison chart between CleanRL's PPO (JAX/XLA + EnvPool) and SEEDRL's R2D2 across 57 Atari games

image_metadata.caption: PPO JAX scan variant: learning curves showing hardware-dependent speed differences.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining JAX-specific optimizations like lax.scan and their trade-offs between compilation time and runtime performance
image_metadata.description: Learning curves (episodic return vs. environment steps) for PPO with JAX/XLA scan variant, comparing performance across contributors with different hardware

image_metadata.caption: PPO JAX scan variant: wall-clock learning curves with hardware variability noted.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the practical considerations of JAX compilation costs and how lax.scan reduces them in RL training loops
image_metadata.description: Learning curves (episodic return vs. wall-clock time) for PPO with JAX/XLA scan variant, highlighting hardware-dependent speed differences between contributors

image_metadata.caption: PPO on ProcGen environments: performance comparison across procedurally generated games.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing ProcGen as a generalization benchmark and comparing PPO's performance to baselines in non-Atari settings
image_metadata.description: Comparison plot of PPO performance on ProcGen environments, likely showing episodic return across multiple procedurally generated games

image_metadata.caption: Training time comparison across PPO variants; color indicates contributor hardware. (CleanRL)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the impact of hardware on RL training speed, or when comparing JAX-based vs. standard PPO implementations in terms of compilation overhead.
image_metadata.description: A learning curve chart comparing training wall-clock time across multiple PPO variants (ppo_atari_envpool_xla_jax.py and related), with runs color-coded by contributor (red for costa-huang, blue/orange for 51616) to highlight hardware-induced speed differences.

image_metadata.caption: Average episodic returns for ppo_procgen.py vs. OpenAI Baselines on Procgen (easy). (CleanRL)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching how to verify RL implementation correctness by comparing against established baselines, or when introducing Procgen as a generalization benchmark.
image_metadata.description: A bar or line chart showing average episodic returns for ppo_procgen.py across multiple Procgen environments (easy distribution), compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO implementation.

image_metadata.caption: Episodic returns vs. wall-clock time for ppo_procgen.py on Procgen easy. (CleanRL)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining the difference between sample efficiency and computational efficiency, or when students need to understand why both time-based and step-based plots are reported.
image_metadata.description: A chart showing average episodic returns for ppo_procgen.py plotted against wall-clock time rather than environment steps, compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO on Procgen easy distribution.

image_metadata.caption: ppo_atari_multigpu.py matches ppo_atari.py sample efficiency across Atari games. (CleanRL)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing multi-GPU RL training or discussing how to scale PPO without losing sample efficiency; useful for comparing single vs. multi-GPU setups.
image_metadata.description: A learning curve chart (episodic return vs. environment steps) comparing ppo_atari_multigpu.py against ppo_atari.py across Atari games, demonstrating equivalent sample efficiency between the single-GPU and multi-GPU implementations.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-GPU PPO achieves ~30% wall-time speedup over single-GPU on Atari. (CleanRL)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the computational benefits of multi-GPU RL training, or when students ask how much speedup can be expected from parallelizing PPO across GPUs.
image_metadata.description: A wall-clock time learning curve comparing ppo_atari_multigpu.py against ppo_atari.py, showing that the multi-GPU variant achieves approximately 30% faster training on the same hardware.

image_metadata.caption: Breakout episodic returns: ppo_atari_multigpu vs. ppo_atari vs. ppo_atari_envpool. (CleanRL)

image_metadata.caption: Agent-environment interaction loop in reinforcement learning. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RL introduction, before defining individual terms, to give students a mental model of the overall system.
image_metadata.description: A circular diagram showing the agent-environment interaction loop: the agent receives observations and rewards from the environment, then sends actions back to the environment, forming a closed feedback cycle.

image_metadata.caption: Symbol 's' denotes the environment state in RL. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first defining the distinction between states and observations, to establish notation before deeper discussion.
image_metadata.description: Inline mathematical symbol 's', representing the state variable in reinforcement learning notation.

image_metadata.caption: Symbol 'o' denotes the agent's observation in RL. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the state symbol when contrasting fully observable vs. partially observable environments.
image_metadata.description: Inline mathematical symbol 'o', representing the observation variable in reinforcement learning notation.

image_metadata.caption: Symbol 'μ' denotes a deterministic policy in RL. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the concept of policies, specifically when distinguishing deterministic from stochastic policies.
image_metadata.description: Inline mathematical symbol 'μ' (mu), used to denote a deterministic policy in reinforcement learning.

image_metadata.caption: Deterministic policy: action at time t is a function of state. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when formally defining deterministic policies, immediately before or after introducing the stochastic policy equation for contrast.
image_metadata.description: Mathematical equation 'a_t = μ(s_t)', expressing that at time t the action is a deterministic function of the current state.

image_metadata.caption: Symbol 'π' denotes a stochastic policy in RL. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing stochastic policies, contrasting with the deterministic 'μ' notation.
image_metadata.description: Inline mathematical symbol 'π' (pi), the standard notation for a stochastic policy in reinforcement learning.

image_metadata.caption: Stochastic policy: action sampled from distribution given state. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show directly after the deterministic policy equation to highlight the contrast between deterministic and stochastic action selection.
image_metadata.description: Mathematical equation 'a_t ~ π(· | s_t)', expressing that at time t the action is sampled from a conditional probability distribution given the current state.

image_metadata.caption: Symbol 'θ' denotes learnable parameters of a policy. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from abstract policies to parameterized (deep learning) policies, before presenting the subscript notation.
image_metadata.description: Inline mathematical symbol 'θ' (theta), representing the parameters (e.g., neural network weights) of a parameterized policy.

image_metadata.caption: Symbol 'φ' is an alternative notation for policy parameters. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside θ when discussing parameterized policies to prevent confusion when students encounter different notation in papers.
image_metadata.description: Inline mathematical symbol 'φ' (phi), an alternative notation for policy parameters in parameterized policies.

image_metadata.caption: Parameterized deterministic and stochastic policies with parameters θ. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing both policy types and the parameter notation, as a synthesis that connects all prior notation into the deep RL setting.
image_metadata.description: A two-line mathematical display showing both parameterized policy forms: 'a_t = μ_θ(s_t)' for deterministic and 'a_t ~ π_θ(· | s_t)' for stochastic, with θ as a subscript denoting parameters.

image_metadata.caption: tanh activation used in deterministic MLP policy network. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing deterministic policies and explaining how neural networks map observations to actions; pair with the MLP code snippet discussion
image_metadata.description: The tanh activation function symbol (\tanh), used as the activation function in the MLP policy network with two hidden layers of size 64

image_metadata.caption: Log-likelihood of action a under stochastic policy \pi_\theta. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When transitioning from deterministic to stochastic policies, and before deriving policy gradient objectives that require computing log-likelihoods
image_metadata.description: The log-likelihood expression \log \pi_{\theta}(a|s), representing the log probability of action a given state s under a parameterized stochastic policy

image_metadata.caption: P_\theta(s): probability vector over discrete actions from categorical policy. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining categorical policies and how the neural network output is converted to action probabilities via softmax; use before the log-likelihood formula
image_metadata.description: The probability vector symbol P_{\theta}(s), denoting the output probability distribution over discrete actions from a categorical policy network given state s

image_metadata.caption: Action 'a' used as index into categorical probability vector. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining how to compute the log-likelihood of a specific action under a categorical policy, just before presenting the indexing formula
image_metadata.description: The symbol 'a' representing a discrete action, used as an index into the probability vector for computing log-likelihood in a categorical policy

image_metadata.caption: Log-likelihood for categorical policy via probability vector indexing. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After introducing P_\theta(s) and the action index 'a'; show this as the key formula students need to implement log-likelihood computation for policy gradient algorithms
image_metadata.description: The complete log-likelihood formula for a categorical policy: \log \pi_{\theta}(a|s) = \log [P_{\theta}(s)]_a, showing that the log-likelihood equals the log of the a-th entry of the probability vector

image_metadata.caption: Covariance matrix \Sigma of multivariate Gaussian policy distribution. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing diagonal Gaussian policies; use to contrast the full covariance matrix with the diagonal simplification that makes the policy tractable
image_metadata.description: The covariance matrix symbol \Sigma, representing the covariance matrix of a multivariate Gaussian distribution used in diagonal Gaussian policies

image_metadata.caption: \mu_\theta(s): neural network mapping observations to mean actions. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining the architecture of diagonal Gaussian policies; contrast with the two approaches for representing the standard deviation
image_metadata.description: The mean action function \mu_{\theta}(s), representing the neural network output mapping observations to mean actions in a diagonal Gaussian policy

image_metadata.caption: Log standard deviation \log\sigma: unconstrained parameterization for Gaussian policy. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing why log stds are preferred over raw stds in Gaussian policies; emphasize the unconstrained optimization benefit
image_metadata.description: The log standard deviation symbol \log \sigma, representing the parameterization choice for the spread of a diagonal Gaussian policy

image_metadata.caption: \log\sigma_\theta(s): state-dependent log std from a neural network. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When comparing the two approaches to representing covariance in Gaussian policies; highlight the trade-off between simplicity (fixed log std) and expressiveness (state-dependent log std)
image_metadata.description: The state-dependent log standard deviation function \log \sigma_{\theta}(s), representing the second approach where a neural network maps states to log standard deviations

image_metadata.caption: Log stds range over (-\infty, \infty), enabling unconstrained optimization. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When justifying the use of log standard deviations; show alongside the \log\sigma notation to explain why this parameterization is preferred in practice
image_metadata.description: The interval (-\infty, \infty), indicating that log standard deviations are unconstrained and can take any real value, unlike standard deviations which must be non-negative

image_metadata.caption: State-dependent standard deviation output by policy network. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing how neural networks output distribution parameters for continuous action spaces; pair with the mean network mu_theta(s) for contrast
image_metadata.description: Mathematical notation for the state-dependent standard deviation function sigma_theta(s), output by a neural network parameterized by theta

image_metadata.caption: Noise vector z used in reparameterized action sampling. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining how actions are sampled from a Gaussian policy using the reparameterization trick; show alongside the full sampling equation
image_metadata.description: Single variable 'z' representing a noise vector sampled from a spherical Gaussian, used in the reparameterization trick for action sampling

image_metadata.caption: Noise z drawn from standard spherical Gaussian N(0, I). (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching the reparameterization trick for Gaussian policies; use to explain why sampling z from N(0,I) and transforming it is equivalent to sampling directly from N(mu, sigma)
image_metadata.description: Mathematical expression z ~ N(0, I), indicating that the noise vector z is sampled from a standard spherical Gaussian distribution

image_metadata.caption: Action sampled via reparameterization: mean plus scaled noise. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching action sampling from Gaussian policies; use as the central equation to explain how mean and standard deviation combine with noise to produce actions
image_metadata.description: Action sampling equation: a = mu_theta(s) + sigma_theta(s) ⊙ z, showing how an action is computed as the mean plus elementwise-scaled noise

image_metadata.caption: ⊙ denotes elementwise (Hadamard) product of two vectors. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When first introducing the action sampling equation a = mu + sigma ⊙ z; clarify this symbol before students encounter the full formula
image_metadata.description: The Hadamard (elementwise) product symbol ⊙, used to denote elementwise multiplication of two vectors

image_metadata.caption: k denotes the dimensionality of the action vector. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the log-likelihood formula for diagonal Gaussian policies; clarify k before presenting the full summation formula
image_metadata.description: Variable 'k' representing the dimensionality of the action space in the log-likelihood formula for a diagonal Gaussian policy

image_metadata.caption: Policy mean mu as neural network output at state s. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When presenting the log-likelihood formula; use to remind students that mu is not a fixed value but a state-dependent neural network output
image_metadata.description: Expression mu = mu_theta(s), defining the mean action as the output of a neural network policy evaluated at state s

image_metadata.caption: Policy standard deviation sigma as neural network output at state s. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When presenting the log-likelihood formula alongside mu = mu_theta(s); emphasize that sigma is also state-dependent and learned
image_metadata.description: Expression sigma = sigma_theta(s), defining the standard deviation as the output of a neural network policy evaluated at state s

image_metadata.caption: Log-likelihood of action under diagonal Gaussian policy. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching policy gradient algorithms that require log-probabilities (e.g., REINFORCE, PPO); use to show how to implement log pi in code and why diagonal Gaussian is computationally tractable
image_metadata.description: Full log-likelihood formula for a k-dimensional action under a diagonal Gaussian policy: log pi_theta(a|s) = -1/2 * (sum over i of [(a_i - mu_i)^2 / sigma_i^2 + 2*log(sigma_i)] + k*log(2*pi))

image_metadata.caption: τ (tau) denotes a trajectory of states and actions. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When first introducing the concept of trajectories in RL; use as a reference symbol before presenting trajectory return formulas or policy gradient objectives
image_metadata.description: Greek letter tau (τ) representing a trajectory — a sequence of states and actions — in reinforcement learning notation

image_metadata.caption: A trajectory τ: alternating states and actions over time. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of introducing trajectories, before discussing transitions or policies, to anchor the formal definition
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered equation defining a trajectory τ as an ordered sequence of alternating states and actions: (s_0, a_0, s_1, a_1, ...)

image_metadata.caption: s₀: the initial state, sampled from the start distribution. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining that the first state in a trajectory is sampled from a start-state distribution, to connect symbol to concept
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered symbol s_0, representing the initial state of the environment at time step zero

image_metadata.caption: ρ₀: the start-state distribution over initial environment states. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside s_0 when explaining how episodes begin, to clarify that the initial state is stochastic
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered symbol ρ_0 (rho subscript 0), denoting the start-state distribution from which the initial state is sampled

image_metadata.caption: Initial state s₀ is sampled from start distribution ρ₀. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing ρ_0 and s_0 separately, to unify them into the full initialization equation
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered equation s_0 ~ ρ_0(·), expressing that the initial state is drawn by sampling from the start-state distribution

image_metadata.caption: t: discrete time step index in a trajectory. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first introducing state transitions to clarify the indexing convention before presenting s_t and s_{t+1}
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered symbol t, representing a discrete time step index in a trajectory

image_metadata.caption: s_t: the environment state at time step t. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining state transitions, pairing it with s_{t+1} to illustrate the before/after relationship
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered symbol s_t, representing the environment state at time step t

image_metadata.caption: t+1: the successor time step following t. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside t when introducing the concept of state transitions to make the temporal relationship explicit
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered expression t+1, representing the next discrete time step after t

image_metadata.caption: s_{t+1}: the next state after action a_t is taken. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when presenting transition dynamics equations, paired with s_t and a_t to illustrate the full transition tuple
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered symbol s_{t+1}, representing the next environment state after taking action a_t in state s_t

image_metadata.caption: a_t: the agent's action at time step t. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing how agents interact with the environment, before presenting transition or policy equations
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered symbol a_t, representing the action taken by the agent at time step t

image_metadata.caption: Deterministic transition: next state is f(s_t, a_t). (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when contrasting deterministic vs. stochastic transitions, as the baseline case before introducing probabilistic transition models
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered equation s_{t+1} = f(s_t, a_t), expressing deterministic state transition dynamics as a function of current state and action

image_metadata.caption: State transition: next state sampled from environment dynamics P. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing how trajectories are generated step-by-step; use to contrast deterministic vs stochastic environments
image_metadata.description: Mathematical equation showing state transition dynamics: s_{t+1} sampled from conditional probability distribution P given current state s_t and action a_t

image_metadata.caption: R denotes the reward function — central to RL objective. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When first introducing the reward function concept; use as a reference anchor before showing the full reward function definitions
image_metadata.description: Single symbol 'R' representing the reward function notation used throughout the reward and return section

image_metadata.caption: General reward function depends on state, action, and next state. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing reward functions; present this as the general case before showing the simplified forms r_t=R(s_t) and r_t=R(s_t,a_t)
image_metadata.description: Equation defining reward r_t as a function R of current state s_t, action a_t, and next state s_{t+1} — the most general form of the reward function

image_metadata.caption: Simplified reward: depends only on current state s_t. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After presenting the general reward form; use to show the state-only simplification and discuss when this assumption is reasonable
image_metadata.description: Equation showing simplified reward function r_t = R(s_t) — reward depends only on the current state

image_metadata.caption: Reward as function of state-action pair — common simplification. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When comparing reward function variants; place between the state-only and full forms to show the progression of complexity
image_metadata.description: Equation showing reward function r_t = R(s_t, a_t) — reward depends on current state and action taken, but not the next state

image_metadata.caption: R(τ) denotes cumulative return over trajectory τ. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When transitioning from per-step reward r_t to cumulative return; use to set up both finite-horizon and infinite-horizon return formulas
image_metadata.description: Notation R(τ) representing the return (cumulative reward) along a trajectory τ — the quantity the agent seeks to maximize

image_metadata.caption: Finite-horizon return: simple sum of rewards over T steps. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing return types; present first as the simpler case before the discounted infinite-horizon version to build intuition
image_metadata.description: Equation for finite-horizon undiscounted return: R(τ) = sum of r_t from t=0 to T — simple sum of rewards over a fixed time window

image_metadata.caption: Discount factor γ ∈ (0,1) ensures convergence of infinite return. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing infinite-horizon discounted return; use to explain why γ<1 is necessary for convergence before showing the full discounted return formula
image_metadata.description: Notation showing discount factor γ (gamma) constrained to the open interval (0,1) — a key hyperparameter in infinite-horizon RL

image_metadata.caption: Infinite-horizon discounted return: future rewards weighted by γ^t. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After introducing γ and finite-horizon return; use to show how discounting solves the infinite-sum convergence problem and discuss the cash-now-vs-later intuition
image_metadata.description: Equation for infinite-horizon discounted return: R(τ) = sum from t=0 to infinity of γ^t * r_t — rewards weighted by exponentially decaying discount factor

image_metadata.caption: T denotes the finite time horizon in episodic RL tasks. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the RL problem setup and expected return; use to clarify the distinction between episodic (finite T) and continuing (infinite horizon) tasks
image_metadata.description: Symbol T representing the time horizon (terminal timestep) in finite-horizon return formulations

image_metadata.caption: Trajectory probability factored over initial state, transitions, and policy. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing how trajectories are distributed under a policy; use before deriving the expected return to ground the expectation in a concrete probability measure.
image_metadata.description: Mathematical formula for the probability of a trajectory τ given policy π, expressed as the product of the initial state distribution and the product over timesteps of transition probabilities and policy probabilities.

image_metadata.caption: J(π): the expected return objective for policy π. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When first defining the RL optimization objective; pair with the full definition of J(π) to anchor the notation before showing the optimization problem.
image_metadata.description: Inline mathematical symbol J(π), denoting the expected return of policy π — the objective function in reinforcement learning.

image_metadata.caption: Expected return J(π) as an integral over trajectory probabilities and returns. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Immediately after presenting the trajectory probability formula; use to show how integrating reward over the trajectory distribution yields the scalar objective J(π).
image_metadata.description: Full definition of J(π) as the integral of trajectory probability times trajectory return, equivalently written as the expectation of R(τ) under trajectories sampled from π.

image_metadata.caption: The RL optimization problem: find π* that maximizes expected return J(π). (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After defining J(π); use as the capstone of the RL problem formulation section to unify trajectory probability, expected return, and the optimization goal.
image_metadata.description: The core RL optimization problem: π* equals the argmax over all policies π of J(π), defining the optimal policy as the one maximizing expected return.

image_metadata.caption: π*: notation for the optimal policy in RL. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When referencing the optimal policy in context; useful as a quick notation reminder when students encounter π* in value function definitions or Bellman optimality equations.
image_metadata.description: Inline symbol π* denoting the optimal policy — the policy that achieves maximum expected return.

image_metadata.caption: V^π(s): on-policy state value function notation. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing value functions; pair with the full definition equation to help students connect the symbol to its mathematical meaning.
image_metadata.description: Inline symbol V^π(s) denoting the on-policy value function — the expected return starting from state s and following policy π.

image_metadata.caption: V^π(s): expected return from state s following policy π. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When formally defining value functions; use to show students how the expectation is conditioned on the initial state, distinguishing V^π from J(π).
image_metadata.description: Full definition of the on-policy value function V^π(s) as the expected return of trajectories sampled from policy π, conditioned on starting in state s.

image_metadata.caption: Q^π(s,a): on-policy action-value function notation. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the Q-function; pair with its full definition to help students distinguish it from V^π(s) by the additional action argument.
image_metadata.description: Inline symbol Q^π(s,a) denoting the on-policy action-value (Q) function — expected return starting from state s, taking action a, then following policy π.

image_metadata.caption: Q^π(s,a): expected return from state s, action a, then policy π. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After defining V^π(s); use to highlight the difference introduced by conditioning on both state and action, and to motivate why Q-functions are useful for action selection.
image_metadata.description: Full definition of the on-policy action-value function Q^π(s,a) as the expected return conditioned on starting in state s, taking action a, then following policy π forever after.

image_metadata.caption: V*(s): optimal state value function — best achievable expected return. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When transitioning from on-policy value functions to optimal value functions; use to set up the Bellman optimality equations and the relationship between V* and Q*.
image_metadata.description: Inline symbol V*(s) denoting the optimal value function — the maximum expected return achievable from state s under the best possible policy.

image_metadata.caption: Optimal value function V*(s): max expected return over all policies. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when first defining optimal value functions, immediately after explaining on-policy value functions V^π(s), to contrast the role of the max operator
image_metadata.description: Mathematical equation defining the Optimal Value Function V*(s) as the maximum over all policies of the expected return R(τ) given that the trajectory starts in state s

image_metadata.caption: Notation for the optimal action-value (Q*) function. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the Q* notation for the first time, before presenting its full definition, to help students associate the symbol with its meaning
image_metadata.description: Inline notation Q*(s,a) representing the Optimal Action-Value Function symbol used as a reference label in the surrounding text

image_metadata.caption: Optimal Q*(s,a): max expected return fixing initial action a. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Present directly after V*(s) definition to highlight the structural difference: Q* conditions on both state and initial action, while V* only conditions on state
image_metadata.description: Mathematical equation defining the Optimal Action-Value Function Q*(s,a) as the maximum over all policies of expected return R(τ), conditioned on starting in state s and taking action a at time 0

image_metadata.caption: V^π(s) as policy-weighted expectation of Q^π(s,a). (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when teaching the connections between value functions; ask students to derive this from definitions before revealing the equation as confirmation
image_metadata.description: Mathematical equation showing that the on-policy value function V^π(s) equals the expectation over actions drawn from policy π of the action-value function Q^π(s,a)

image_metadata.caption: V*(s) equals max over actions of Q*(s,a). (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Present alongside the V^π–Q^π relationship to contrast on-policy averaging with optimal maximization; use to motivate the concept of greedy action selection
image_metadata.description: Mathematical equation showing that the optimal value function V*(s) equals the maximum over actions a of the optimal action-value function Q*(s,a)

image_metadata.caption: Q*: the optimal action-value function symbol. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Surface when discussing the relationship between Q* and optimal action selection, as a notation anchor before presenting the argmax formula
image_metadata.description: Inline symbol Q* representing the optimal action-value function, used as a reference in the surrounding text about the connection between Q* and the optimal policy

image_metadata.caption: Notation a*(s) for the optimal action at state s. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when explaining that knowing Q* is sufficient to derive the optimal policy, before showing the argmax formula for a*(s)
image_metadata.description: Inline symbol a*(s) representing the optimal action as a function of state, used as a reference label in the surrounding text

image_metadata.caption: Optimal action a*(s) = argmax over Q*(s,a). (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Present after introducing Q* and V*=max_a Q*(s,a); use to motivate why many RL algorithms focus on learning Q* rather than the policy directly
image_metadata.description: Mathematical equation defining the optimal action a*(s) as the argmax over actions a of the optimal action-value function Q*(s,a)

image_metadata.caption: Bellman equations for V^π and Q^π: reward plus discounted future value. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce after students understand V^π and Q^π definitions; use to explain why value functions can be estimated iteratively and how TD methods work
image_metadata.description: System of two Bellman equations for on-policy value functions: V^π(s) expressed as expected immediate reward plus discounted next-state value, and Q^π(s,a) expressed as expected immediate reward plus discounted expected next action-value

image_metadata.caption: Notation: next state s' sampled from transition distribution P. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Bellman equations to explain the notation before students encounter it in the full equations, preventing confusion about what s' ~ P means
image_metadata.description: Shorthand notation 's' ~ P indicating that the next state s' is sampled from the environment's transition probability distribution P

image_metadata.caption: Shorthand: next state s' sampled from environment transition rules. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing Bellman equations and a student asks what the expectation subscript means or how the next state is determined.
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered mathematical notation showing s' ~ P(·|s,a), representing that the next state s' is sampled from the environment's transition probability distribution conditioned on current state s and action a.

image_metadata.caption: s' denotes the next state after a transition. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When a student is confused about the difference between s (current state) and s' (next state) in Bellman equation derivations.
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered symbol s', representing the next state variable in the context of Bellman equation notation.

image_metadata.caption: Shorthand: action a sampled from policy π. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining on-policy Bellman equations and students need to understand how actions are selected under a given policy.
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered shorthand notation a ~ π, indicating that action a is sampled from the policy π.

image_metadata.caption: Full form: action a drawn from policy conditioned on state s. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When a student needs to understand the full meaning behind the shorthand a ~ π in Bellman equations, especially when first encountering policy notation.
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered full notation a ~ π(·|s), showing that action a is sampled from the policy distribution conditioned on the current state s.

image_metadata.caption: Shorthand: next action a' sampled from policy π. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the on-policy Q-function Bellman equation and students need to distinguish between current action a and next action a'.
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered shorthand notation a' ~ π, indicating that the next action a' is sampled from the policy π.

image_metadata.caption: Full form: next action a' drawn from policy conditioned on next state s'. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When deriving the on-policy Q-function Bellman equation and students need to see the full expanded form of the next-action sampling notation.
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered full notation a' ~ π(·|s'), showing that the next action a' is sampled from the policy conditioned on the next state s'.

image_metadata.caption: Bellman optimality equations for V* and Q* with max over actions. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching the distinction between on-policy and optimal value functions, or when introducing dynamic programming and Q-learning foundations.
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered system of two Bellman optimality equations: V*(s) = max_a E[r(s,a) + γV*(s')] and Q*(s,a) = E[r(s,a) + γ max_a' Q*(s',a')], defining the optimal value function and optimal action-value function.

image_metadata.caption: The 'max' operator distinguishes optimal from on-policy Bellman equations. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When a student asks why optimal Bellman equations look different from on-policy ones, or when explaining why optimal policies are deterministic.
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered 'max' operator symbol, highlighting the key mathematical operation that distinguishes optimal Bellman equations from on-policy Bellman equations.

image_metadata.caption: A^π(s,a): advantage function measuring relative action quality under policy π. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the advantage function concept, especially before discussing policy gradient algorithms like A2C or PPO that rely on advantage estimates.
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered notation A^π(s,a), representing the advantage function for policy π evaluated at state s and action a.

image_metadata.caption: π(·|s): policy as a conditional distribution over actions given state s. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the advantage function or policy gradient methods, to remind students that the policy is a full distribution over actions, not just a single action.
image_metadata.description: LaTeX-rendered notation π(·|s), representing the policy as a conditional probability distribution over actions given state s.

image_metadata.caption: Advantage function: how much better action a is than average. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce this equation immediately after explaining Q and V functions, when transitioning to why relative action quality matters more than absolute value in policy gradient methods.
image_metadata.description: Mathematical equation defining the advantage function: A^π(s,a) = Q^π(s,a) - V^π(s), showing it as the difference between the action-value function and the state-value function.

image_metadata.caption: The MDP 5-tuple: the formal foundation of all RL environments. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first introducing the formal MDP framework, as an overview before unpacking each component individually.
image_metadata.description: Mathematical notation for the MDP 5-tuple: ⟨S, A, R, P, ρ₀⟩, representing the formal components of a Markov Decision Process.

image_metadata.caption: S: the set of all valid states in an MDP. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when enumerating each element of the MDP 5-tuple, specifically when defining what the state space represents.
image_metadata.description: Symbol S representing the set of all valid states (state space) in an MDP.

image_metadata.caption: A: the set of all valid actions in an MDP. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when enumerating each element of the MDP 5-tuple, specifically when defining what the action space represents.
image_metadata.description: Symbol A representing the set of all valid actions (action space) in an MDP.

image_metadata.caption: Reward function R maps state-action-next-state triples to real numbers. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when defining the reward component of the MDP, especially to clarify that reward can depend on the transition outcome, not just the action.
image_metadata.description: Mathematical definition of the reward function R: S × A × S → ℝ, mapping state-action-next-state triples to real-valued rewards.

image_metadata.caption: Reward at time t depends on current state, action, and next state. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the reward function definition (Image 5) to connect the abstract mapping to its practical use during environment interaction.
image_metadata.description: Equation r_t = R(s_t, a_t, s_{t+1}) showing how the reward at timestep t is computed from the current state, action, and next state.

image_metadata.caption: Transition function P maps state-action pairs to distributions over next states. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining environment dynamics and the Markov property, to illustrate that the next state is probabilistically determined by only the current state and action.
image_metadata.description: Mathematical definition of the transition probability function P: S × A → 𝒫(S), mapping state-action pairs to probability distributions over next states.

image_metadata.caption: P(s'|s,a): probability of reaching s' from state s with action a. (OpenAI Spinning Up)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside Image 7 as the practical shorthand notation, and when discussing the Markov property to reinforce that history beyond (s,a) is irrelevant.
image_metadata.description: Conditional probability notation P(s'|s,a) representing the probability of transitioning to next state s' given current state s and action a.

image_metadata.caption: CliffWorld grid environment: agent navigates from start to goal avoiding cliff. (Neuromatch Academy)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the transition from single-state bandit problems to multi-state RL problems, specifically before explaining why Q-learning is needed.
image_metadata.description: A 2D grid world environment called 'CliffWorld' showing a grid with a starting state, goal state, cliff tiles, and navigable tiles. The agent must traverse the grid from start to goal while avoiding falling off the cliff.

image_metadata.caption: Q-learning results: Q-table heatmap and learning metrics after training in CliffWorld. (Neuromatch Academy)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after students implement the Q-learning update rule, to help them interpret and validate their results and understand what a converged Q-table looks like.
image_metadata.description: Four-panel visualization of a trained Q-learning agent in CliffWorld: (1) top-left shows the Q-table as a heatmap across states and actions, highlighting that moving right from start or down above the cliff has very negative values; (2) other panels likely show learning curves, episode rewards, and/or policy visualization over training.

image_metadata.caption: SARSA learning results in CliffWorld: on-policy TD updates yield a safer learned path. (Neuromatch Academy)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after students implement SARSA and run it on CliffWorld, prompting comparison with Q-learning results to highlight behavioral differences near the cliff.
image_metadata.description: Visualization output comparing SARSA algorithm performance in CliffWorld, likely showing Q-table values, episode rewards, and/or the learned policy path. Illustrates how SARSA's on-policy updates (using epsilon-greedy next action) differ from Q-learning's off-policy updates (using max Q-value).
